papers.title: A Neuroevolution Approach to Keypoint-Based Sign Language Fingerspelling Classification
papers.abstract: Non-verbal communication frameworks such as Sign Language and Makaton serve as a vital means of communication for millions of people with hearing impairments. The development of accurate and efficient recognition systems for non-verbal communication is of great importance towards fostering inclusion through accessible systems. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to improving fingerspelling recognition through the application of neuroevolution as a means to hyperheuristically improve deep neural networks. We propose the use of these algorithms to optimise the classification of low-dimensional datasets given the mixed levels of computational resources in the community setting. A dataset of 1678 images comprised of seven subjects performing ASL fingerspelling is processed into normalised keypoints, and three neuroevolution simulations are executed to search the problem space for the most effective topology. The results show that the simulation finds a promising set of hyperparameters, achieving a mean 10-fold cross-validation accuracy of 97.44% by using a total of 1478 hidden units within four layers. Our neuroevolution approach demonstrates remarkable potential for the enhancement of finger-spelling recognition in non-verbal communication systems, paving the way for more inclusive technologies in the future.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method using neuroevolution to enhance fingerspelling recognition in sign language. The primary contribution is the application of this method to a dataset of ASL fingerspelling images, which aligns with the criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the execution of three neuroevolution simulations to optimize a classification model for fingerspelling recognition, indicating hands-on experimental work. It also mentions the processing of a dataset of 1678 images and reports a mean accuracy of 97.44% from 10-fold cross-validation, which are clear empirical results.

papers.title: Co-design of Gesture-Based Arabic Sign Language (ArSL) Recognition
papers.abstract: #tldr: SignSpeak provides a framework for two-way communication by translating ArSL into spoken language and transcribing spoken language into text, and an Arabic sign language (ArSL) recognition system using a leap motion controller.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a framework for recognizing Arabic Sign Language (ArSL) and translating it into spoken language, which involves a computational method for sign language recognition. The primary contribution is the development of a recognition system that targets sign language, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an Arabic sign language recognition system using a leap motion controller, which implies hands-on experimental work. Although it does not provide specific details about experiments or performance metrics, the mention of a recognition system indicates that there is likely an implementation and evaluation involved, making it empirical.

papers.title: ADAT novel time-series-aware adaptive transformer architecture for sign language translation
papers.abstract: Current sign language machine translation systems rely on recognizing hand movements, facial expressions, and body postures, and natural language processing, to convert signs into text. While recent approaches use Transformer architectures to model long-range dependencies via positional encoding, they lack accuracy in recognizing fine-grained, short-range temporal dependencies between gestures captured at high frame rates. Moreover, their quadratic attention complexity leads to inefficient training. To mitigate these issues, we introduce ADAT, an Adaptive Transformer architecture that combines convolutional feature extraction, log-sparse self-attention, and an adaptive gating mechanism to efficiently model both short- and long-range temporal dependencies in sign language sequences. We evaluate ADAT on three datasets: the benchmark RWTH-PHOENIX-Weather-2014 (PHOENIX14T), the ISL-CSLTR, and the newly introduced MedASL, a medical-domain American Sign Language corpus. In sign-to-gloss-to-text translation, ADAT outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines, improving BLEU-4 by at least 0.1% and reducing training time by an average of 21% across datasets. In sign-to-text translation, ADAT consistently surpasses transformer-based encoder-decoder baselines, achieving a minimum of 0.5% gains in BLEU-4 and an average training speedup of 21.8% across datasets. Compared to the encoder-only and decoder-only baselines in sign-to-text, ADAT is at least 0.7% more accurate, despite being up to 12.1% slower due to its dual-stream structure.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method, the ADAT architecture, specifically designed for sign language translation. It addresses the challenges of recognizing temporal dependencies in sign language sequences and demonstrates improvements in translation accuracy and training efficiency on multiple datasets. This aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the introduction of the ADAT architecture, which is evaluated on three datasets. It reports improvements in BLEU-4 scores and training time reductions, indicating that experiments were conducted to compare ADAT against state-of-the-art baselines. This hands-on evaluation and the use of multiple datasets clearly demonstrate that the work is empirical.

papers.title: Machine Learning Approach for Real Time Translation of Sinhala Sign Language into Text
papers.abstract: #tldr: This research is aimed at creating a real time Sinhala sign language translator by identifying letter-based signs using image processing and machine learning techniques and is able to identify 26 hand gestures by using the CNN network.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a machine learning approach to translate Sinhala sign language into text by identifying letter-based signs through image processing and a convolutional neural network. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract indicates that the research involves creating a real-time translator that identifies letter-based signs using machine learning techniques, specifically mentioning the use of a CNN network to identify 26 hand gestures. This implies hands-on experimental work with model implementation and evaluation, making it empirical.

papers.title: Breaking The Communication Barrier: Leveraging Xai And Imaging For Deaf Gesture Recognition
papers.abstract: Ignorance of the wider population in sign language severely limits the possibilities of communication between the people who are hearing-impaired or are deaf and those who have normal hearing while consequentially influencing the deaf community negatively. This paper dwells on the deep learning-based approach for the understanding of sign language (SL) gestures, to provide a way through the communication problem between hearing and deaf individuals. We performed the work based on two datasets, including the newly introduced Sign Language MNIST dataset, which is patterned after the classic MNIST format but focused on American Sign Language (ASL) gestures. The Sign Language MNIST dataset offers a multi-class problem with 24 letter classes, excluding J and Z, and was created using an image pipeline to expand the original hand gesture images with diverse modifications. We utilized a properly created Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify different types of hand motions in sign language. The second dataset used in this paper is also sign language consisting of various ASL movements. The approach we developed with the customized CNN model can be concluded as an excellent tool for recognizing the hand gestures of the deaf community, as it exhibited a 94% accuracy, 95% precision, 94% recall, and 94% F1 – score for the dataset of ASL gestures, which represents a clear improvement over the already existing technologies. For the dataset of multi-class, we achieved an accuracy of 96% and the results are illustrated in table 1. In the same way, we implemented Explainable AI (XAI) techniques which created Density Heatmaps that gave an extent to the interpretability of the CNN Model's decision-making process for hand gestures. Heatmaps that were produced add a lot of credibility to the features that were done as a whole aid to recognizing sign gestures. Through a vivid understanding of the results of our research, it is evident that the approach we selected is very effective in accurately representing sign gestures. It boasted an impressive track record, setting the benchmark with high percentages of both accuracy and performance. Future work includes enhancing the model performance by incorporating more diverse datasets, implementing advanced preprocessing techniques, and integrating other XAI methods for deeper insight into the model. This study contributes to the emerging field of SL recognition, offering an effective model for gesture recognition and insight into its interpretability.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning-based approach for recognizing sign language gestures using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model. It introduces a new dataset (Sign Language MNIST) specifically for American Sign Language gestures and demonstrates the effectiveness of the model in classifying these gestures, which aligns with the criteria for computational methods in sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the development and evaluation of a CNN model for recognizing sign language gestures, utilizing two datasets, including the newly introduced Sign Language MNIST dataset. It reports performance metrics such as 94% accuracy, 95% precision, and 94% recall, indicating empirical evaluation of the model's effectiveness. The implementation of Explainable AI techniques further supports the empirical nature of the work by providing insights into the model's decision-making process.

papers.title: Deep Sign: Enabling Robust Statistical Continuous Sign Language Recognition via Hybrid CNN-HMMs
papers.abstract: This manuscript introduces the end-to-end embedding of a CNN into a HMM, while interpreting the outputs of the CNN in a Bayesian framework. The hybrid CNN-HMM combines the strong discriminative abilities of CNNs with the sequence modelling capabilities of HMMs. Most current approaches in the field of gesture and sign language recognition disregard the necessity of dealing with sequence data both for training and evaluation. With our presented end-to-end embedding we are able to improve over the state-of-the-art on three challenging benchmark continuous sign language recognition tasks by between 15 and 38% relative reduction in word error rate and up to 20% absolute. We analyse the effect of the CNN structure, network pretraining and number of hidden states. We compare the hybrid modelling to a tandem approach and evaluate the gain of model combination.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method for continuous sign language recognition by integrating a CNN with an HMM in an end-to-end framework. The primary contribution is focused on improving the performance of sign language recognition systems, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a hybrid CNN-HMM model for continuous sign language recognition and reports improvements over state-of-the-art results, including specific reductions in word error rates. It also mentions comparisons and evaluations of different model structures and configurations, indicating that this work is empirical and data-driven.

papers.title: Deep Learning Framework for Indian Sign Language Communication
papers.abstract: This research presents a deep learning-based framework for the real-time recognition and translation of Indian Sign Language (ISL) gestures into both text and spoken words. The goal is to improve communication between individuals who are deaf or hard-of-hearing and those who can hear. The framework uses a combined computer vision pipeline, integrating image preprocessing, keypoint extraction, and data augmentation to improve model performance. We collected a curated ISL dataset, which includes both isolated signs and continuous gesture sequences. Data collection followed standardized guidelines on lighting, background uniformity, signer variability, and class balance to ensure effectiveness in real-world conditions. For recognition, we created a hybrid neural network that uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to extract important spatial features from hand shapes and body postures, along with temporal models like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (3D-CNNs) to capture motion dynamics. The training strategy includes transfer learning, adaptive learning rates, early stopping, and dropout methods to reduce overfitting and enhance model performance. We used accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis as evaluation metrics for isolated and continuous sign recognition tasks. Experimental results on our dataset show high accuracy (over 98%) for isolated sign recognition and competitive performance for continuous sign sequences. This highlights the model's ability to handle both static and dynamic gestures. We implemented a prototype application for both desktop and mobile platforms, achieving real-time speeds and smooth translation from ISL to text and speech. These results suggest the system can serve as a practical communication tool with potential use in education, accessibility, and public services.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning framework specifically designed for the real-time recognition and translation of Indian Sign Language gestures into text and spoken words. The primary contribution includes a computational method for sign language recognition, along with the creation of a curated dataset for training and evaluation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a deep learning framework for recognizing and translating Indian Sign Language gestures, detailing the creation of a curated dataset and the implementation of a hybrid neural network. It reports on various evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, indicating that experiments were conducted to assess the model's performance. The mention of achieving over 98% accuracy for isolated sign recognition further supports that this is empirical work.

papers.title: Advanced Glove for Deaf and Dumb with Speech and Text Message on Android Cell Phone
papers.abstract: Most of us are blessed with natural ability to see, listen and interact. However, few people do not have this ability. Due to various reasons like birth defects, accidents, oral diseases, and environmental impacts the number of such people is rapidly increasing in the recent years. Most of the deaf and dumb use gesture and sign symbol for the communication. Sign language is the form of communication where shapes, arms or body, orientation and movement of hands, and facial expressions are systematically combined. But, sign language is ineffective in many cases. It may not be sufficient when such people are involved in the educational, social and professional environments where they want to interact with others. Therefore, there is an immediate need to have an advanced sign language detection and gesture recognition system. The need, design methodology and implementation strategies of a smart glove are discussed in this paper. The main objective of the work is to bring the technology for the help of differently abled people to communication effectively and in most meaningful form. The hand glove converts the hand/finger gestures in to meaningful voice messages over an Android phone. An app is designed using Android Studio for the said purpose. There are several works carried out across the globe for converting hand and finger gesture or sign language into speech. The work described in this paper uses NodeMCU as processing and wireless communication of data and it is most advanced form of all the hand glove designs so far.

filter_explanations.area: This paper discusses the design and implementation of an advanced glove that detects sign language gestures and converts them into voice messages. While it mentions the use of a smart glove, the primary contribution appears to be the computational method for gesture recognition and communication, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sensor-based sign recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the design and implementation of a smart glove for sign language detection and gesture recognition, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions the use of NodeMCU for processing and wireless communication, which implies a practical implementation of the system. Although specific results or metrics are not provided, the focus on design methodology and implementation strategies suggests empirical work.

papers.title: Mime Recognition for Indian Sign Language
papers.abstract: Signs are part of non-verbal conveyance. Deaf and mute people primarily use sign language to interact with one another. Communicating with them has always been a significant challenge for the general public, as gestures are difficult to understand. Therefore, the important idea is to assist the com- munication absence among the public and the hearing impaired. Various sign language systems have been developed, but they are neither flexible nor cost-effective. Therefore, this project proposes an effective and user-friendly sign language recognition interface that makes it easy for hearing-impaired people to communicate with the general public using Tensorflow, Keras, and CNN (convolutional neural networks) for gesture recognition.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for recognizing Indian Sign Language gestures using a user-friendly interface built with Tensorflow, Keras, and CNNs. The primary contribution is a sign language recognition system, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract mentions the development of a sign language recognition interface using Tensorflow, Keras, and CNN for gesture recognition, indicating that the authors are implementing a system. Although specific experimental results or metrics are not provided, the use of these frameworks and the focus on gesture recognition imply hands-on, experimental work.

papers.title: Sinhala Sign Language Detection Approach for Deaf People Using Human Pose Estimation
papers.abstract: People with disabilities have physical, sensory, cognitive, or behavioral health problems that limit their participation in activities and interaction with the environment. Deafness is one of the most common types of disability in society. Since verbal communication is impossible for the deaf, sign language is one of the most often used forms of communication among those disable people. However, a third party who is fluent in sign language is necessary for efficient communication. This research study proposed a sign language detector for Sinhala using Logistic Regression, Ridge Classifier, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting algorithms with human pose estimation. The method used upper-body stance landmarks with 100 human pose videos and 40 widely used signals to extract features for body language classification. With an overall 86.75% success rate, this study is a major step toward improving deaf people's communication in Sri Lanka.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for detecting Sinhala sign language using various machine learning algorithms in conjunction with human pose estimation. The primary contribution is the development of a sign language recognition system, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a sign language detection system using various algorithms and human pose estimation. It mentions the use of 100 human pose videos and 40 signals for feature extraction, indicating hands-on experimental work. The reported success rate of 86.75% further supports that this is empirical research.

papers.title: An Efficient Speech to Sign Language Conversion and Text Recognition through Live Gesture
papers.abstract: Despite advancements in technology, a significant portion of the global population (over 5%) continues to face communication barriers due to deafness and speech impairments. Existing solutions often lack the comprehensiveness and inclusivity required to address the diverse needs of this community. Traditional methods, relying solely on speech-to-text or text-to-sign language conversion, fail to facilitate seamless two-way communication, limiting interpersonal connections and fostering a sense of isolation. Our research proposes a novel, multimodal approach integrating speech-to-sign language conversion and live gesture-to-text recognition, leveraging audio and visual inputs. By combining these modalities, our system aims to bridge the communication gap, enabling real-time, bidirectional interactions between individuals with and without hearing or speech impairments. Employing speech recognition, natural language processing techniques, and machine learning models developed using Python, our solution incorporates a custom dataset, encompassing personalized hand symbols and offering the flexibility to choose between American Sign Language (ASL) and Indian Sign Language (ISL), promoting inclusivity and catering to cultural preferences. The necessity of this research lies in its potential to revolutionize assistive technologies, fostering a more connected and empathetic society. By providing a comprehensive platform that facilitates effective communication and mutual understanding, our solution improves quality of life, educational opportunities, and social inclusion for individuals with impairments.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel multimodal approach for converting speech to sign language and recognizing gestures in real-time. The primary contribution is a computational method that integrates speech recognition and gesture recognition to facilitate communication for individuals with hearing or speech impairments, which aligns with the criteria for sign language recognition and production.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a multimodal approach that integrates speech-to-sign language conversion and live gesture-to-text recognition, indicating the development of a system. It mentions the use of machine learning models and a custom dataset, which implies hands-on experimental work. Although specific results or metrics are not provided, the focus on building and implementing a solution for real-time communication suggests empirical research.

papers.title: Maldivian Sign Language Recognition Application
papers.abstract: Maldivian Sign Language (MVSL) is the national sign language for the deaf or the hearing-impaired people in Maldives. These people with disabilities could easy communicate with other deaf people but face great difficulty conversing with ordinary people. There are many recognition applications and models made for many sign languages with different approaches. Sadly, no such technology has been made to recognize MVSL signs. Therefore, this research aims to recognize three dynamic MVSL signs (“water”, “promise” and “read”) in real-time and later expand to recognize more signs to create a translation application for MVSL. We propose an approach with MediaPipe holistic to extract key point landmarks from the face, two hands and the pose from the videos and build the model for recognition using LSTM. The model was able to obtain an accuracy of 93%.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for recognizing Maldivian Sign Language (MVSL) signs using a model built with MediaPipe and LSTM. The primary contribution is the development of a sign language recognition application that targets specific signs, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a recognition model for Maldivian Sign Language using MediaPipe and LSTM, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions achieving an accuracy of 93%, which is a performance metric that supports the empirical nature of the research.

papers.title: BdSLW-11: Dataset of Bangladeshi sign language words for recognizing 11 daily useful BdSL words
papers.abstract: The dataset of Bangladeshi sign language words (BdSLW) is rare. Though there are lots of datasets of BdSL sign alphabets, numbers, or characters, there are not enough datasets of sign words. This is the first dataset about sign words of BdSL according to the author(s) knowledge. So, this dataset is developed by collecting data from people. This is an image dataset. This dataset is a collection of 1105 images of sign words. A total of 11 sign word categories are selected which are important and daily use in our life. As this is an image dataset, so the images of sign words are taken by camera from the sign users of Bangladesh. Authors have gone to the individuals of sign users and captured images from them with their permission. Then the images are analyzed and segmented into the images which have quality such as no background, clear, bright, etc. This dataset is used for recognizing BdSL sign words.

filter_explanations.area: The primary contribution of this paper is the creation of a new dataset specifically for recognizing Bangladeshi sign language words, which is intended for training or evaluating sign language recognition systems. This aligns with the IN SCOPE criterion of providing new datasets/corpora for sign language processing.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper introduces a new dataset of Bangladeshi sign language words, which is developed through data collection from sign users. The creation of this dataset implies empirical work as it involves gathering and analyzing images for the purpose of recognizing sign words, making it a data-driven contribution.

papers.title: Real time Hand Gesture Recognition using different algorithms based on American Sign Language
papers.abstract: #excerpt: Human Computer Interaction (HCI) is a broad research field based on human interaction with computers or machines. Basically, Hand Gesture Recognition (HGR) is a subfield of HCI. Today, many researchers are working on different HGR applications like game controlling, robot control, smart home system, medical services etc. The purpose of this paper is to represent a real time HGR system based on American Sign Language (ASL) recognition with greater accuracy. This system acquires gesture images of ASL with black background from mobile video camera for feature extraction. In the processing phase, the system extracts five features such as fingertip finder, eccentricity, elongatedness, pixel segmentation and rotation. For feature extraction, a new algorithm is proposed which basically combines K curvature and convex hull algorithms. It can be called 'K convex hull' method which can detect fingertip with high accuracy. In our system, Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is used with feed forward,

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a real-time hand gesture recognition system specifically targeting American Sign Language (ASL). The primary contribution includes a new algorithm for feature extraction and the use of an Artificial Neural Network for recognition, which aligns with the criterion of computational methods for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a real-time hand gesture recognition system for American Sign Language, detailing the feature extraction process and the use of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for recognition. The introduction of a new algorithm for feature extraction and the mention of accuracy improvements imply experimental work and evaluation, making this paper empirical.

papers.title: Multi-Semantic Discriminative Feature Learning for Sign Gesture Recognition Using Hybrid Deep Neural Architecture
papers.abstract: The speech and hearing-impaired community use sign language as the primary means of communication. It is quite challenging for the general population to interpret or learn sign language completely. A sign language recognition system must be designed and developed to address this communication barrier. Most current sign language recognition systems rely on wearable sensors, keeping the recognition system unaffordable for most individuals. Moreover, the existing vision-based sign recognition frameworks do not consider all of the spatial and temporal information required for accurate recognition. A novel vison-based hybrid deep neural net methodology is proposed in this study for recognizing Indian and Russian sign gestures. The proposed framework is aimed to establish a single framework for tracking and extracting multi-semantic properties, such as non-manual components and manual co- articulations. Furthermore, spatial feature extraction from the sign gestures is deployed using a 3D deep neural net with atrous convolutions. The temporal and sequential feature extraction is carried out by employing attention-based Bi-LSTM. In addition, the distinguished abstract feature extraction is done using the modified autoencoders. The discriminative feature extraction for differentiating the sign gestures from unwanted transition gestures is done by leveraging the hybrid attention module. The experimentation of the proposed model has been carried out on the novel multi-signer Indo-Russian sign language dataset. The proposed sign language recognition framework with hybrid neural net yields better results than other state-of-the-art frameworks.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel vision-based hybrid deep neural network methodology specifically designed for recognizing Indian and Russian sign gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition by extracting multi-semantic features, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a novel vision-based hybrid deep neural network methodology for sign gesture recognition, which includes experimentation on a new dataset. It mentions that the proposed framework yields better results than existing state-of-the-art frameworks, indicating an empirical evaluation of the model's performance.

papers.title: A Sign Language Interactive System based on Multi-feature Fusion
papers.abstract: The sign language interaction system not only helps improve the communication mode of deaf-mutes, but also helps us explore gesture interaction technology. In this paper, the MYO armband was used to collect 6-channel inertial signals and 8-channel sEMG signals, and 8 subjects were invited to establish 35 Chinese sign language data sets, and a complete sign language interactive system was developed with a mobile application. In the aspect of algorithm, a segmentation method based on the comprehensive judgment of average energy and volatility is proposed to extract the starting and ending points of active segments more accurately. In addition, a multi-feature fusion method is proposed in feature selection, and the accuracy of the best subset is 98.12% verified by SVM classifier. At the same time, it is determined that the classification effect is better when the feature dimension is about 30.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a sign language interactive system that includes a computational method for segmenting and classifying sign language gestures using multi-feature fusion and SVM classification. The primary contribution is the algorithmic approach to processing sign language data, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods related to sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the development of a sign language interactive system, including the collection of data from 8 subjects to create a dataset. It also proposes a segmentation method and reports an accuracy of 98.12% verified by an SVM classifier, indicating empirical evaluation of the system's performance.

papers.title: State Space Models are Effective Sign Language Learners: Exploiting Phonological Compositionality for Vocabulary-Scale Recognition
papers.abstract: Sign language recognition suffers from catastrophic scaling failure: models achieving high accuracy on small vocabularies collapse at realistic sizes. Existing architectures treat signs as atomic visual patterns, learning flat representations that cannot exploit the compositional structure of sign languages-systematically organized from discrete phonological parameters (handshape, location, movement, orientation) reused across the vocabulary. We introduce PHONSSM, enforcing phonological decomposition through anatomically-grounded graph attention, explicit factorization into orthogonal subspaces, and prototypical classification enabling few-shot transfer. Using skeleton data alone on the largest ASL dataset ever assembled (5,565 signs), PHONSSM achieves 72.1% on WLASL2000 (+18.4pp over skeleton SOTA), surpassing most RGB methods without video input. Gains are most dramatic in the few-shot regime (+225% relative), and the model transfers zero-shot to ASL Citizen, exceeding supervised RGB baselines. The vocabulary scaling bottleneck is fundamentally a representation learning problem, solvable through compositional inductive biases mirroring linguistic structure.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents PHONSSM, a computational method for sign language recognition that leverages phonological compositionality to improve performance on large vocabularies. The primary contribution is a novel model that addresses the challenges of scaling in sign language recognition, making it fit within the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the introduction of a new model (PHONSSM) that is trained on a large dataset of 5,565 signs, achieving specific performance metrics such as 72.1% accuracy and an 18.4 percentage point improvement over previous methods. It also discusses the model's performance in few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, indicating empirical evaluation and comparison with existing methods. This clearly demonstrates hands-on experimental work, making it an empirical study.

papers.title: LSF-ANIMAL: A Motion Capture Corpus in French Sign Language Designed for the Animation of Signing Avatars
papers.abstract:
Signing avatars allow deaf people to access information in their preferred language using an interactive visualization of the sign language spatio-temporal content. However, avatars are often procedurally animated, resulting in robotic and unnatural movements, which are therefore rejected by the community for which they are intended. To overcome this lack of authenticity, solutions in which the avatar is animated from motion capture data are promising. Yet, the initial data set drastically limits the range of signs that the avatar can produce. Therefore, it can be interesting to enrich the initial corpus with new content by editing the captured motions. For this purpose, we collected the LSF-ANIMAL corpus, a French Sign Language (LSF) corpus composed of captured isolated signs and full sentences that can be used both to study LSF features and to generate new signs and utterances. This paper presents the precise definition and content of this corpus, technical considerations relative to the motion capture process (including the marker set definition), the post-processing steps required to obtain data in a standard motion format and the annotation scheme used to label the data. The quality of the corpus with respect to intelligibility, accuracy and realism is perceptually evaluated by 41 participants including native LSF signers.

Keywords: Corpus, Motion Capture, French Sign Language, Marker Set, Annotation, Perceptual Evaluation

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents the LSF-ANIMAL corpus, which is a dataset of motion capture data for French Sign Language. The primary contribution is the creation of a new dataset intended for training or evaluating systems that animate signing avatars, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criterion for new datasets/corpora.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper introduces the LSF-ANIMAL corpus, which is a dataset of motion capture data for French Sign Language. It also reports on a perceptual evaluation of the corpus's quality in terms of intelligibility, accuracy, and realism, indicating an experimental evaluation of the dataset. This hands-on work with a dataset and the evaluation of its quality supports the conclusion that the paper is empirical.

papers.title: The Serbian Sign Language Alphabet: A Unique Authentic Dataset of Letter Sign Gestures
papers.abstract: Language barriers and the communication difficulties of individuals with developmental disabilities are two major causes of communication problems that societies worldwide encounter. A particularly challenging group is hearing-impaired people who have difficulties with communication, reading, writing, learning, and social interactions, which have a substantial impact on their quality of life. This article focuses on detailing a Serbian Sign Language alphabet database and the method for creating it in order to provide a foundation for answering the various societal challenges of persons who use the Serbian language. In front of a computer camera, 41 people performed Serbian Sign Language sign movements that replicated the Serbian alphabet for this study’s aims. Hand and body key points were identified using the recorded video clips, and the numerical values of the identified key points were then stored in a database for further processing. In total, 8.346 video clips of people making recognized hand gestures were gathered, processed, classed, and archived. This paper provides a thorough technique that may be applied to comparable tasks and details the process of constructing a dataset based on Serbian Sign Language alphabet signs. This dataset was created using custom-made Python 3.11 software. Data regarding dynamic video clips that capture entire subject movement were incorporated into this dataset to fill in the gaps in other similar efforts based on static photographs. Thus, the purpose of this investigation is to employ innovative technology to support the community of hearing-impaired people in areas such as general inclusion, education, communication, and empowerment.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a unique dataset of Serbian Sign Language alphabet gestures, detailing the method of its creation and the processing of video clips to identify hand and body key points. The primary contribution is the development of a new dataset intended for training or evaluating systems related to sign language, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the creation of a dataset of Serbian Sign Language gestures, detailing the process of collecting and processing 8,346 video clips. This involves hands-on experimental work in building a dataset, which is a key component of empirical research, even though it does not report specific performance metrics or results.

papers.title: A new algorithm and other software for disambiguation of polysemy and homonymy for computer translation into Russian Sign Language based on a semantic principle

Карла Маркса , 20 , Новосибирск , 630073 , Россия 2 Новосибирский государственный педагогический университет ул . filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a new algorithm for disambiguating polysemy and homonymy in the context of translating into Russian Sign Language. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language translation through semantic analysis, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the development of software and an algorithm for semantic analysis and translation into Russian Sign Language, indicating hands-on work. It mentions a comparative analysis of existing systems and the introduction of a new model, which implies experimental evaluation of the proposed approach. This suggests empirical work in the context of software development and algorithm implementation.

papers.title: HYBRID CNN-LSTM WITH SOFT ATTENTION FOR REAL-TIME ASL GESTURE RECOGNITION USING MEDIAPIPE LANDMARKS
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a real-time American Sign Language (ASL) recognition system using a hybrid deep learning model that integrates 1D Convolutional Neural Networks(Conv1D), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) layers, and a custom soft attention mechanism. The model processes 60-frame sequences of 3D pose and hand landmarks (258 features per frame) extracted using Mediapipe, allowing the system to learn both spatial and temporal gesture patterns without relying on raw image input. A custom dataset comprising 6,800 gesture sequences across 17 classes—including 16 ASL signs and an idle state—was curated using spatial data augmentation techniques such as mirroring and rotational transformations to ensure robustness across diverse users, lighting, and angles. The model achieved a high testing accuracy of 95.88%, with 96.87% precision and 95.88% recall, and significantly outperformed GRU, RNN, and CNN-RNN baselines in comparative evaluations. Despite its architectural complexity, the system performs efficiently on low-power hardware such as a MacBook Air M2, enabling real-time inference suitable for integration into live video conferencing platforms. The proposed solution offers a scalable, lightweight, and accessible approach to gesture-based communication, advancing the practicality of deep learning in inclusive assistive technologies. Index Terms—LSTM, GRU, RNN, ASL Gesture, BiLSTM, Mediapipe

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a hybrid deep learning model for real-time American Sign Language (ASL) gesture recognition, which is a computational method specifically targeting sign language. The primary contribution includes the development of a model that processes 3D pose and hand landmarks for recognizing ASL gestures, along with the creation of a custom dataset for training and evaluation. This aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the development of a hybrid CNN-LSTM model for real-time ASL gesture recognition, detailing the training on a custom dataset of 6,800 gesture sequences. It reports high testing accuracy, precision, and recall, as well as comparative evaluations against other models, indicating empirical evaluation and performance metrics.

papers.title: Machine-Learning-Assisted Soft Fiber Optic Glove System for Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Sign language recognition devices are effective approaches to breaking the communication barrier between signers and non-signers and exploring human-machine interactions. Wearable gloves have been developed for gesture recognition and virtual reality applications by employing flexible sensors for motion detection and machine learning for data analysis. However, most existing wearable devices present limited sign language translating capacity due to the sensors' design and distribution. Here, we propose a cost-effective dual-hand soft fiber optic glove system consisting of multimode soft liquid-core fiber optic sensors, gyroscopes, wireless printed circuit boards, and batteries for sign language translation. In combination with different deep learning techniques and recognition strategies, the glove system can recognize static gestures and dynamic gestures of American Sign Language, and deduce the meaning of sentences by the sequence of gestures. The soft glove system exhibits a broad sign language range (10 numbers, 26 alphabets, 18 words, and 5 sentences meaning prediction), and high recognition accuracy (98.6% for static gestures, 95% for dynamic gestures). The results also present the recognizing capacity for high-correlated gestures (e.g., “M” and “N”). Finally, we demonstrate its application for controlling the motion of a virtual character through 7 discrete commands in the VR interface.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel soft fiber optic glove system that utilizes machine learning techniques for recognizing both static and dynamic gestures in American Sign Language. The primary contribution is a computational method that enhances sign language recognition capabilities, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria for sensor-based sign recognition where the modeling and algorithm are the main focus.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a soft fiber optic glove system that utilizes machine learning techniques for recognizing both static and dynamic gestures in American Sign Language. It reports high recognition accuracy for these gestures and discusses the system's ability to deduce meanings from sequences of gestures, indicating hands-on experimental work and evaluation of the system's performance.

papers.title: Real-Time Sign Language Converter for Mute and Deaf People
papers.abstract: Deaf people may get irritated due to the problem of not being able to share their views with common people, which may affect their day-to-day life. This is the main reason to develop such system that can help these people and they can also put their thoughts forward similar to other people who don’t have such problem. The advancement in the Artificial intelligence provides the door for developing the system that overcome this difficulty. So this project aims on developing a system which will be able to convert the speech to text for the deaf person, and also sometimes the person might not be able to understand just by text, so the speech will also get converted to the universal sign language. Similarly, for the mute people the sign language which they are using will get converted to speech. We will take help of various ML and AI concepts along with NLP to develop the accurate model. Convolutional neural networks (CNN) will be used for prediction as it is efficient in predicting image input, also as lip movements are fast and continuous so it is hard to capture so along with CNN, the use of attention-based long short-term memory (LSTM) will prove to be efficient. Data Augmentation methods will be used for getting the better results. TensorFlow and Keras are the python libraries that will be used to convert the speech to text. Currently there are many software available but all requires the network connectivity for it to work, while this device will work without the requirement of internet.Using the proposed model we got the accuracy of 100% in predicting sign language and 96% accuracy in sentence level understanding.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system that converts speech to text and sign language, utilizing machine learning techniques such as CNN and LSTM for real-time sign language conversion. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at facilitating communication for deaf and mute individuals, which aligns with the criteria for sign language recognition and production.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a system that converts speech to text and sign language, utilizing CNNs and LSTMs for prediction. It mentions the use of data augmentation and reports accuracy metrics of 100% for sign language prediction and 96% for sentence-level understanding, indicating hands-on experimental work and evaluation of the model's performance.

papers.title: American Sign Language Translation via Smart Wearable Glove Technology
papers.abstract: In the world of sound, for those without it, sign language is a powerful tool to make their voices heard. The ASL, short for American Sign Language is the most widely used sign language across the globe with certain variations according to the country. In this work, we developed a wearable wireless gesture decoder module that can translate the fundamental set of ASL gestures into corresponding alphabets and words. Our project utilizes a glove that houses a series of flex sensors on the metacarpal and inter-phalangeal joints of the fingers to detect the bending of fingers, through piezoresistive effect. The glove is outfitted with an accelerometer as well, that helps to detect the hand movements. Simple classification algorithms from machine learning are then applied to translate the gestures into alphabets or words. With the assistance of a bluetooth module, the data is sent wirelessly. The result is then displayed and heard on the android smartphone using an android application named “Dastaana”, which is an Urdu word for glove. Portability, compactness, cost-effectiveness and a user-friendly android application gives this system an edge.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a wearable glove technology that translates American Sign Language gestures into corresponding alphabets and words using machine learning algorithms. The primary contribution is a computational method that involves gesture recognition and translation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition and production.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a wearable gesture decoder module that translates ASL gestures into alphabets and words. It mentions the use of machine learning classification algorithms to process the data from the glove's sensors, indicating hands-on experimental work. The implementation of a system that translates gestures and the application of machine learning algorithms suggest that this is empirical work.

papers.title: Comparative Evaluation and Utilization of Convolutional Neural Network Architectures for Irish Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Irish Sign Language (ISL) stands as a preferred mode of communication used by the deaf and hard-of-hearing community in Ireland. With its unique grammar, syntax, and lexicon, ISL plays a pivotal role in facilitating communication for thousands of individuals, reflecting centuries of cultural heritage and linguistic development. An estimated 5,000 Deaf individuals utilize ISL, with an additional 40,000 hearing individuals, spanning from regular to occasional users, also engaging with Irish Sign Language. Despite its cultural and linguistic importance, ISL faces numerous challenges in terms of technical accessibility. The exclusion of sign languages from modern language technologies places the deaf or hard-of-hearing individuals at a disadvantage, exacerbating the barrier to human-to-human communication and further marginalizing an already under-resourced linguistic subset. This necessitates innovative approaches and technologies to enhance its utilization and promote inclusivity. This research is concerned with evaluating the performance of various deep neural network architectures in the recognition of sign language by utilizing and evaluating state-of-the-art architectures for the recognition of sign language, in particular Irish Sign Language. This research is part of research progress towards the development of an automatic computational annotation system for Irish Sign Language. Notably, the Densenet architecture performed better than other architectures in ISL alphabet recognition with an average accuracy of 99\%. Our findings illustrate the potential of sophisticated deep neural networks to overcome constraints relating to the scarcity of ISL-specific data. This contribution provides the potential to further develop natural language processing tools and technologies for Irish Sign Language, which may alleviate the lack of technical communicative accessibility and inclusion for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community in Ireland.

filter_explanations.area: This paper evaluates various deep neural network architectures specifically for the recognition of Irish Sign Language (ISL). The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the evaluation of various deep neural network architectures for recognizing Irish Sign Language, specifically mentioning that the Densenet architecture achieved an average accuracy of 99%. This indicates that experiments were conducted to compare the performance of different models, making this empirical work.

papers.title: HIERARCHICAL MULTIMODAL TRANSFORMER FOR SIGN LANGUAGE RECOGNITION
papers.abstract:
Traditional sign language recognition (SLR) systems primarily focus on hand gestures, while facial expressions and body posture also play a crucial role in solving these
problems.
This paper presents a multimodal transformer architecture (MM-Transformer) that
integrates three main aspects of sign language: hand gestures, facial expressions, and body posture. The proposed system has a hierarchical fusion mechanism that combines
specialized encoders: 3D-CNN for hand gesture recognition, a deep residual network for
facial expression analysis, and a keypoint tracking system for body posture estimation.
Testing results show that this system achieves 93.2% accuracy. The proposed model results in higher inference time and memory consumption compared to models that process only hand gestures. However, it achieves higher inference accuracy while maintaining real-time performance.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a multimodal transformer architecture specifically designed for sign language recognition, integrating hand gestures, facial expressions, and body posture. The primary contribution is a computational method that enhances the recognition of sign language by utilizing a hierarchical fusion mechanism, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a multimodal transformer architecture for sign language recognition, detailing the use of specialized encoders for different aspects of sign language. It reports a testing result of 93.2% accuracy, indicating empirical evaluation of the system's performance. The comparison of inference time and memory consumption further supports the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: BenSignNet: Bengali Sign Language Alphabet Recognition Using Concatenated Segmentation and Convolutional Neural Network
papers.abstract: Sign language recognition is one of the most challenging applications in machine learning and human-computer interaction. Many researchers have developed classification models for different sign languages such as English, Arabic, Japanese, and Bengali; however, no significant research has been done on the general-shape performance for different datasets. Most research work has achieved satisfactory performance with a small dataset. These models may fail to replicate the same performance for evaluating different and larger datasets. In this context, this paper proposes a novel method for recognizing Bengali sign language (BSL) alphabets to overcome the issue of generalization. The proposed method has been evaluated with three benchmark datasets such as `38 BdSL’, `KU-BdSL’, and `Ishara-Lipi’. Here, three steps are followed to achieve the goal: segmentation, augmentation, and Convolutional neural network (CNN) based classification. Firstly, a concatenated segmentation approach with YCbCr, HSV and watershed algorithm was designed to accurately identify gesture signs. Secondly, seven image augmentation techniques are selected to increase the training data size without changing the semantic meaning. Finally, the CNN-based model called BenSignNet was applied to extract the features and classify purposes. The performance accuracy of the model achieved 94.00%, 99.60%, and 99.60% for the BdSL Alphabet, KU-BdSL, and Ishara-Lipi datasets, respectively. Experimental findings confirmed that our proposed method achieved a higher recognition rate than the conventional ones and accomplished a generalization property in all datasets for the BSL domain.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method (BenSignNet) for recognizing Bengali sign language alphabets, utilizing a combination of segmentation, augmentation, and a CNN-based classification approach. The primary contribution is a method specifically designed for sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and evaluation of a novel method for recognizing Bengali sign language alphabets using a CNN-based model. It reports performance accuracy on three benchmark datasets and claims that the proposed method achieves a higher recognition rate than conventional methods, indicating empirical evaluation and comparison.

papers.title: Development of a Sign Language Recognition System Using Machine Learning
papers.abstract: Deafness and voice impairment have been persistent disabilities throughout history, hindering individuals from engaging in verbal communication and leading to their isolation from the predominantly vocally communicating society. Sign language has emerged as the primary mode of communication for people with these disabilities. However, it presents a language barrier as it is not commonly understood by those who can hear. To address this issue, various methods for recognizing sign language have been proposed. This paperaims to develop a machine learning-based system that can recognize sign language in real-time. The paper involved the acquisition of a dataset consisting of 44,654 images representing the static American Sign Language (ASL) alphabet signs. The HandDetector module was utilized to detect and capture images of the signer's hand forming each sign through a PC webcam. The dataset was split into three sets: training data (20,772 cases), validation data (8,903 cases), and test data (14,979 cases). Image pre-processing techniques were implemented on the images and a convolutional neural network (CNN) model was trained and compiled. The CNN utilized in the paper comprised of three convolutional layers and a SoftMax output layer and it was compiled using the Adam optimizer and categorical cross-entropy loss function. The performance of the system was evaluated using the test dataset. Notably, the system achieved remarkable accuracy rates, having a training accuracy of 99.86%, a validation accuracy of 99.94%, and a test accuracy of 94.68%. The results obtained from this study demonstrated significant advancements in sign language recognition, surpassing previous findings in the literature.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a machine learning-based system for recognizing American Sign Language (ASL) signs using a convolutional neural network (CNN). The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the development of a machine learning-based sign language recognition system, detailing the acquisition of a large dataset, the training of a CNN model, and the evaluation of its performance with specific accuracy rates. This indicates hands-on experimental work, making it empirical as it involves training a model and reporting performance metrics.

papers.title: Intelligent Learning Systems for Inclusive Education in Ghana: Towards an Effective Engagement with Hard of Hearing Students
papers.abstract: This research attempts to address some challenges faced when facilitating an inclusive classroom experience for Deaf/Hard of Hearing students (D/HH) in Ghanaian schools and universities by proposing an intelligent learning system. Hence, this research contributes an Intelligent and Inclusive Learning System (IILS) with two main components: signWithMe subsystem and audio-visual transcription system (AVTS) subsystem.The signWithMe subsystem presents a Ghanaian sign language learning management system (LMS) that is motivated by the limited sign language literacy in Ghana. This subsystem consists of a sign language dictionary, an E-resource, an E-forum, and an E-payment system. It supports inclusive learning of the Ghanaian sign language for both Deaf and Non-Deaf students to interact with the system. This ensures improved diversity and inclusion in our Ghanaian society. Also, the AVTS focuses on improving the teaching and learning experience of Deaf students in Ghanaian schools. The AVTS contributes a novel artificial intelligence approach that uses a counter-checking of Google speech-to-text and lip-reading-to-text transcriptions of the Ghanaian sign language in inclusive university lectures. These two subsystems provide services in line with the mandate of the tenth and fourth Sustainable Development Goal (S.D.G.) of “Reduced Inequality” and “Quality Education” respectively.Finally, the IILS was analysed per the two main components (i.e., signWithMe and AVTS) and are considered to have high level of usability by 20 Deaf and 5 Non-Deaf users.

filter_explanations.area: The paper presents an Intelligent and Inclusive Learning System (IILS) that includes a sign language learning management system (signWithMe) and an audio-visual transcription system (AVTS). The signWithMe subsystem specifically targets the learning and interaction with Ghanaian sign language, which aligns with the criterion of providing a resource for sign language education. Therefore, the primary contribution is computational in nature and relevant to sign language processing.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an Intelligent and Inclusive Learning System (IILS) that includes a sign language learning management system and an audio-visual transcription system. It mentions the implementation of these subsystems and their evaluation based on usability feedback from users, indicating hands-on experimental work. This suggests empirical research as it involves the creation and testing of a system designed to improve educational experiences for Deaf and Hard of Hearing students.

papers.title: Sign Language Recognition for Speech and Hearing Impaired by Image Processing in MATLAB
papers.abstract: The paper presents the model of a sign language interpreter that can verbalize American Sign Language (ASL). This robust model is based on creating a human-computer interface (HCI) using the user's hand gesture only. The combination of Hardware and software interfaces-webcam and MATLAB 2016a-performs the feature extraction process from the image captured from real-time video of hand signs. These features are compared with the features of the database images and after some image processing techniques in MATLAB, the system generates outputs depending on the prediction of highest resemblance. As the model is free from any other apparatus or accessories, it is solely practical and easy to use. This model provided satisfactory accuracy in our tests without any need of any constant or unicolor background. The proposed technique, together with a vast source database, will definitely be highly beneficial for mitigating the communication gap between the people with speaking and hearing abilities and those without them.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational model for sign language recognition that utilizes image processing techniques in MATLAB to interpret American Sign Language (ASL) gestures. The primary contribution is a system that processes hand gestures captured via a webcam to generate verbal outputs, which fits the criterion of a computational method for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a model for sign language interpretation that involves feature extraction from real-time video and compares these features with a database. It mentions testing the model and achieving satisfactory accuracy, indicating that experiments were conducted to evaluate the system's performance. This hands-on work with a practical implementation makes it empirical.

papers.title: Image Translation of Bangla and English Sign Language to Written Language using Convolutional Neural Network
papers.abstract: One particular thing that differentiates humans from other species is their abilities to interact. to communicate with others, humans invented languages as units. There are 6500 languages in this world and English has been established as a global language. However, there are a ton of physically disabled human beings who are deprived of expressing their emotions through verbal language. Therefore, sign language has been discovered by expressing feelings with the help of signs which is mainly done by moving body parts: hands in particular. Although, it is so rare to find a research where both Bangladeshi Sign Language (BdSL) and American Sign Language (ASL) is translated, previously some of the prominent researchers worked on primary or secondary ASL and BdSL datasets separately and obtained high accuracy (> 97%) based on their algorithmic approaches. The executed system focuses on implementing both of the aforementioned sign languages combinedly as well as working on both the primary and secondary datasets using single algorithmic approach in order to resolve the two way communication and a better understanding of communicating through sign language. In this thesis, the advantages of real world pictures of Bangladeshi Sign Languages will be used to run an algorithm which will convert sign language to written language using Sequential Convolutional Neural Network method. The system will be able to detect both BdSL and ASL regarding any background with the accuracy of 95.23% and 98.45% respectively.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method using a Sequential Convolutional Neural Network to translate Bangladeshi Sign Language (BdSL) and American Sign Language (ASL) into written language. The primary contribution is the algorithmic approach to sign language recognition and translation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a Sequential Convolutional Neural Network to translate Bangladeshi Sign Language and American Sign Language into written language. It mentions the use of real-world pictures and reports accuracy metrics of 95.23% and 98.45% for the respective sign languages, indicating that this work involves hands-on experimental evaluation and data-driven results.

papers.title: Comparative Study of Pre-Trained Models for BISINDO Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Sign language recognition (SLR) technology is crucial for connecting with individuals who are hearing impaired. However, it's incredibly challenging to create accurate SLR for less common languages like Indonesian Sign Language (BISINDO) because there simply aren't enough large datasets available. A hopeful solution is to use a transfer learning approach. In our study, we looked at three popular pretrained models, namely MobileNetV2, VGG19, and YOLOv8, to see how well they could recognize static BISINDO alphabet signs. We measured their performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a collection of static BISINDO images. The results showed that YOLOv8 was significantly better than MobileNetV2 and VGG19, achieving an accuracy of $\mathbf{9 3 \%}$ compared to their $\mathbf{5 9 \%}$ and $\mathbf{5 5 \%}$. YOLOv8's integrated preprocessing and optimized design made it better at classifying, while MobileNetV2 and VGG19 seemed to overfit the data. These findings really show how effective YOLOv8 is for BISINDO recognition and offer important insights for future efforts to improve SLR technology for sign languages that don't have many resources. Our findings provide a critical baseline for BISINDO sign language recognition and offer strong evidence for the effectiveness of modern, real-time architectures in resource-constrained scenarios, addressing a significant gap in assistive technology for the Indonesian Deaf community.

filter_explanations.area: This paper evaluates the performance of three pre-trained models (MobileNetV2, VGG19, and YOLOv8) for recognizing static signs in BISINDO, which is a sign language. The primary contribution is a comparative analysis of computational methods for sign language recognition, fitting the criterion of evaluating systems intended for sign language processing.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a comparative study of three pre-trained models for recognizing BISINDO sign language, reporting performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. It clearly indicates that experiments were conducted to evaluate the models, with specific results showing that YOLOv8 outperformed the others. This hands-on evaluation and the use of performance metrics support the conclusion that this is empirical work.

papers.title: An Efficient Approach for Interpretation of Indian Sign Language using Machine Learning
papers.abstract: Non-verbal communication involves the usage of Sign Language. The sign language is used by people with hearing / speech disabilities to express their thoughts and feelings. But normally, people find it difficult to understand the hand gestures of the specially challenged people as they do not know the meaning of the sign language gestures. Usually, a translator is needed when a speech / hearing impaired person wants to communicate with an ordinary person and vice versa. In order to enable the specially challenged people to effectively communicate with the people around them, a system that translates the Indian Sign Language (ISL) hand gestures of numbers (1-9), English alphabets (A-Z) and a few English words to understandable text and vice versa has been proposed in this paper. This is done using image processing techniques and Machine Learning algorithms. Different neural network classifiers are developed, tested and validated for their performance in gesture recognition and the most efficient classifier is identified.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system that translates Indian Sign Language gestures into text and vice versa, utilizing image processing techniques and machine learning algorithms. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development, testing, and validation of different neural network classifiers for gesture recognition, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions the identification of the most efficient classifier, which implies performance evaluation and comparison, making this an empirical study.

papers.title: AI based sign language recognition system
papers.abstract: This study presents an AI-based system designed to facilitate communication between hearing-impaired and hearing individuals by translating sign language gestures into spoken English. The proposed system recognizes and decodes sign language motions captured through video inputs by utilizing deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The input video is first processed by the system using a variety of CNN models that have been trained to extract features. After identification, the signs are translated into appropriate text, which is then converted into speech by a Text-to-Speech (TTS) engine. The model can recognize various hand shapes, movements, and facial expressions, which are essential for accurate sign language interpretation, after being trained on a large dataset of annotated sign language gestures. Due to its real-time operation, the technology provides an effective communication method for individuals with hearing impairments. This approach offers a feasible solution for improving accessibility in social interactions, healthcare, education, and customer service by significantly reducing the communication barrier between hearing-impaired and hearing individuals.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents an AI-based sign language recognition system that utilizes deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks, to recognize and translate sign language gestures into spoken English. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an AI-based sign language recognition system that utilizes CNNs, indicating that the model has been trained on a large dataset of annotated sign language gestures. The mention of feature extraction, real-time operation, and the system's ability to recognize various hand shapes, movements, and facial expressions suggests hands-on experimental work. Therefore, this paper is empirical as it involves training a model and implementing a system for practical use.

papers.title: Real-Time Sign Language Recognition and Communication: Leveraging CNN for Deaf and Mute Communities
papers.abstract: Accessibility, communication, and empowerment issues are common for people with sensory disabilities. Although this problem has been the subject of many studies, there is no one-stop application. The accessibility platform is a platform-independent application accessible through a website. This initiative seeks to assist in resolving these issues through the real-time translation of Indian sign language into text. Additionally, it would provide users the opportunity to connect with NGOs that focus on empowering people with disabilities. It is web-based making it platform-independent. The system aims to create a comprehensive, all-in-one feature platform, rather than focusing on individual features. The Indian Sign Language feature uses a Convolutional Neural Network as the algorithm for image classification, it uses Leaky ReLu and Adams optimizers. The use of these optimizers ensures that the accuracy of the research reaches above 90 percent in comparison to the accuracy of other networks that use sigmoid and tanh activation functions. The system achieved an accuracy of 97 percent in real time with the data set of 1200 images and 35 classes i.e. 26 alphabets and 9 numbers.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a real-time sign language recognition system that utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for translating Indian sign language into text. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a real-time sign language recognition system that utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for image classification. It reports achieving 97% accuracy with a dataset of 1200 images across 35 classes, indicating hands-on experimental work and performance evaluation. The mention of optimizers and comparisons to other networks further supports the empirical nature of the research.

papers.title: Deep JSLC: A Multimodal Corpus Collection for Data-driven Generation of Japanese Sign Language Expressions
papers.abstract:
The three-dimensional visualization of spoken or written information in Sign Language (SL) is considered a potential tool for better inclusion of deaf or hard of hearing individuals with low literacy skills. However, conventional technologies for such CG-supported data display are not able to depict all relevant features of a natural signing sequence such as facial expression, spatial references or inter-sign movement, leading to poor acceptance amongst speakers of sign language. The deployment of fully data-driven, deep sequence generation models that proved themselves powerful in speech and text applications might overcome this lack of naturalness. Therefore, we collected a corpus of continuous sentence utterances in Japanese Sign Language (JSL) applicable to the learning of deep neural network models. The presented corpus contains multimodal content information of high resolution motion capture data, video data and both visual and gloss-like mark up annotations obtained with the support of fluent JSL signers. Furthermore, all annotations were encoded under three different encoding schemes with respect to directions, intonation and non-manual information. Currently, the corpus is employed to learn first sequence-to-sequence networks where it shows the ability to train relevant language features.

Keywords: sign language, deep learning corpus, assistive language technologies

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a multimodal corpus specifically designed for the data-driven generation of Japanese Sign Language expressions. The primary contribution is the creation of a new dataset that is intended for training deep learning models, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criterion of developing resources for sign language processing systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the collection of a multimodal corpus specifically for training deep neural network models, indicating hands-on data-driven work. It mentions the use of this corpus to learn sequence-to-sequence networks, which implies experimental evaluation of the models on the collected data. This involvement with a dataset and the training of models supports the conclusion that the work is empirical.

papers.title: Empowering Communication: Harnessing CNN and Mediapipe for Sign Language Interpretation
papers.abstract: Sign language serves as a crucial communication medium for the Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing community. However, the lack of automated systems for sign language recognition can limit interaction with people who are unfamiliar with it. This study proposes a real-time sign language detection system leveraging OpenCV for image preprocessing, MediaPipe for hand tracking, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for gesture classification. The system captures video feed, isolates hand regions, and identifies gestures that correspond to alphabets in American Sign Language (ASL). Through this model, it has achieved a promising classification accuracy, thereby showing significant promise for real-world applications. This work aims to bridge the communication gap by facilitating seamless interaction between hearing impaired individuals and the broader community, enhancing inclusivity and accessibility.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for real-time sign language detection using CNNs and MediaPipe for hand tracking. The primary contribution is the development of a system that recognizes gestures corresponding to American Sign Language, which fits the criterion of sign language recognition.
