filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a real-time sign language detection system that utilizes CNN for gesture classification, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions the use of image preprocessing and hand tracking, and while it states that the model achieved promising classification accuracy, it does not provide specific metrics. However, the implementation of a system and the mention of gesture classification imply empirical evaluation.

papers.title: Sign Language Recognition Using ResNet50 Deep Neural Network Architecture
papers.abstract: Communication is a barrier between the deaf-mute community and the rest of the society. Sign Language is used for communication among such people who cannot speak and listen. The automation of sign language recognition has gained researchers attention in the last few years. Many complex and costly hardware systems have already been developed to assist the purpose. However, we propose to use deep learning approach for automated sign language recognition. We devised a novel 2-level ResNet50 based Deep Neural Network Architecture to classify fingerspelled words. The dataset used is the standard American Sign Language Hand gesture dataset by [1]. The dataset was first augmented using various augmentation techniques. In our 2-level ResNet50 based approach the Level 1 model classifies the input image into one of the 4 sets. After an image is classified into one of the sets it is provided as an input to the corresponding second level model for predicting the actual class of the image. Our approach yields an accuracy of 99.03% on 12,048 test images.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel deep learning architecture (2-level ResNet50) specifically designed for automated sign language recognition, focusing on classifying fingerspelled words. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition accuracy, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a novel 2-level ResNet50 deep neural network architecture for classifying fingerspelled words in sign language. It mentions the use of a standard dataset, the augmentation of this dataset, and reports an accuracy of 99.03% on a substantial number of test images. This indicates hands-on experimental work, making it empirical.

papers.title: Real-Time Sign Language Recognition with MediaPipe Hand Landmarks and Random Forest Classification
papers.abstract: In order to improve communication mobility for people with communication problems, this study presents a realtime sign language recognition system. The technique provides dependable and effective performance by using a Random Forest algorithm for gesture categorization and MediaPipe for efficient hand feature extraction. A well-structured dataset was selected and improved using preprocessing and data augmentation approaches to increase resilience against a variety of situations, including individual variances, hand orientations, and illumination fluctuations. According to evaluation findings, the suggested method outperforms conventional models like logistic regression, support vector machines, and convolutional neural networks, with a recognition accuracy of 93.0 %. By converting sign gestures into both text and speech outputs in real time, which is suitable for use in domains like education, healthcare, and assistive technologies.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a real-time sign language recognition system that utilizes a Random Forest algorithm for gesture classification and MediaPipe for hand feature extraction. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a real-time sign language recognition system using a Random Forest algorithm and MediaPipe for feature extraction. It mentions the use of a well-structured dataset, preprocessing, and data augmentation, indicating hands-on experimental work. Additionally, it reports evaluation findings that show the method outperforms conventional models, providing a clear empirical basis for the claims made.

papers.title: Communication through Indian Sign Language: An Approach for Data Preprocessing
papers.abstract: People who are specially impaired with regards to speaking and hearing find it difficult to communicate with people who are not impaired. The same is true vice-versa. For this very reason, Sign Language is necessary to be known and understood by both the parties. However, there are many people who are completely unaware of Sign Language and its importance. Hence, this project is aimed at bridging this communication gap between the two parties, and eventually, have an awareness on this importance of Sign Language. The paper presents an introductory analysis for the development of the Indian Sign Language (ISL) Recognition System. The primary focus of the paper is to discuss various steps involved in the formation, preprocessing, and augmentation of the dataset. The paper also briefly discusses the various steps performed after preprocessing to train and implement the model, with the focus on the systems based on Artificial Neural Networks.

filter_explanations.area: This paper discusses the preprocessing and augmentation of a dataset for Indian Sign Language recognition, which is a crucial step in developing a computational method for sign language recognition. While it focuses on data preparation, it is part of the broader context of building a recognition system, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the preprocessing and augmentation of a dataset for an Indian Sign Language Recognition System and mentions training and implementing a model based on Artificial Neural Networks. This indicates hands-on experimental work, making the paper empirical.

papers.title: Kenyan Sign Language (KSL) Dataset: Using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Bridging Communication Barrier among the Deaf Learners
papers.abstract: Kenyan Sign Language (KSL) is the primary language used by the deaf community in Kenya. It is the medium of instruction from Pre-primary 1 to university among deaf learners, facilitating their education and academic achievement. Kenyan Sign Language is used for social interaction, expression of needs, making requests and general communication among persons who are deaf in Kenya. However, there exists a language barrier between the deaf and the hearing people in Kenya. Thus, the innovation on AI4KSL is key in eliminating the communication barrier. Artificial intelligence for KSL is a two-year research project (2023-2024) that aims to create a digital open-access AI of spontaneous and elicited data from a representative sample of the Kenyan deaf community. The purpose of this study is to develop AI assistive technology dataset that translates English to KSL as a way of fostering inclusion and bridging language barriers among deaf learners in Kenya. Specific objectives are: Build KSL dataset for spoken English and video recorded Kenyan Sign Language and to build transcriptions of the KSL signs to a phonetic-level interface of the sign language. In this paper, the methodology for building the dataset is described. Data was collected from 48 teachers and tutors of the deaf learners and 400 learners who are Deaf. Participants engaged mainly in sign language elicitation tasks through reading and singing. Findings of the dataset consisted of about 14,000 English sentences with corresponding KSL Gloss derived from a pool of about 4000 words and about 20,000 signed KSL videos that are either signed words or sentences. The second level of data outcomes consisted of 10,000 split and segmented KSL videos. The third outcome of the dataset consists of 4,000 transcribed words into five articulatory parameters according to HamNoSys system.

filter_explanations.area: The primary contribution of this paper is the creation of a comprehensive dataset for Kenyan Sign Language (KSL), which includes video recordings and transcriptions of KSL signs. This dataset is intended for training and evaluating AI systems that translate English to KSL, thus fitting the criterion of new datasets/corpora intended for such systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the creation of a dataset for Kenyan Sign Language, detailing the data collection process and the outcomes, including a large number of signed videos and transcriptions. This indicates hands-on experimental work in building a dataset, which is a key component of empirical research.

papers.title: Harnessing Vision Transformers for Indian Sign Language Sentence Recognition From Videos
papers.abstract: Signwave is an assistive technology designed to bridge communication gaps for people with hearing and speech impairments. Instead of recognizing just individual signs, it can interpret continuous gestures in real time and turn them into complete sentences. To make this possible, the system combines a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for capturing spatial features with a Vision Transformer (ViT) for modeling temporal patterns, allowing it to understand even complex gestures. Our system achieved an accuracy of 72.9%, along with solid precision, recall, and F1-score performance. With both text and speech outputs, Signwave has the potential to be useful in many settings, ranging from classrooms and healthcare to work environments and everyday conversations.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method (Signwave) that utilizes a combination of CNN and Vision Transformer for recognizing Indian Sign Language sentences from videos. The primary contribution is focused on sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a system called Signwave that combines CNN and ViT for recognizing Indian Sign Language sentences from videos. It reports an accuracy of 72.9% and mentions performance metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score, indicating that this is empirical work involving experimental evaluation.

papers.title: American Sign Language Alphabet Recognition using Leap Motion Controller
papers.abstract: #tldr: A method for ASL alphabet recognition with use of a Leap Motion Controller with use of a Convolutional Neural Network for visual feature extraction and an ASL alphabet dataset is established, on which the proposed model achieves prediction accuracies.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a method for recognizing the American Sign Language alphabet using a Leap Motion Controller and a Convolutional Neural Network for feature extraction. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract mentions the use of a Convolutional Neural Network for visual feature extraction and indicates that a dataset for ASL alphabet recognition is established. It also implies that the model achieves prediction accuracies, suggesting that experiments were conducted to evaluate the model's performance, making this empirical work.

papers.title: Dynamic Sign Language Recognition Based on CBAM with Autoencoder Time Series Neural Network
papers.abstract: The CNN-LSTM network has a low generalization ability, and the backward relevance of actions is not strong. In this work, a convolutional self-encoding timing network with a fusion of attention mechanism, namely, convolutional block attention module (CBAM), is proposed. The model first designs a convolutional self-encoding network for pretraining to obtain feature vectors of smaller dimensions. Second, it uses the BN network to speed up the training process and enhance the network generalization ability. Then, we use the encoder part of the pretrained convolutional autoencoder, embed the attention mechanism to further focus on the weight of important parts in image features, and use Bi-LSTM to form a CNN-Bi-LSTM network. As compared with the traditional CNN-LSTM model, the proposed method continuously expands the training samples through the pretrained network to improve the generalization performance. The experimental results show that the method proposed in this paper effectively recognizes the sign language video. The recognition rate reaches 89.90%, which is higher as compared to other methods. These results verify the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method for dynamic sign language recognition using a convolutional self-encoding timing network combined with an attention mechanism and Bi-LSTM. The primary contribution is the development of a new model that improves recognition performance on sign language videos, which fits the criterion for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a CNN-Bi-LSTM network for sign language recognition, detailing the use of a convolutional self-encoding network for pretraining and the application of an attention mechanism. It reports an experimental recognition rate of 89.90%, indicating hands-on experimental work and a comparison with traditional methods, which supports the empirical nature of the research.

papers.title: Acquisition and Classification System of EMG Signals for Interpreting the Alphabet of the Sign Language
papers.abstract: —Taking into account that in Peru, there is an increase in people with difficulties in speaking or communicating. According to the National Institute of Statistics and Informatics of Peru (INEI for its acronym in Spanish), around 80000 people use the gesturing language. For this reason, this research proposes to use the electromyography (EMG) signals to detect the hand movement and identify the alphabet of the sign language to provide essential communication to people who need it. The idea is to classify the signals and recognize the letters of the Spanish alphabet, interpreted in the Peruvian sign language. The results show the classification of the 27 letters of the alphabet with a general success rate of 93.9%.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system that uses electromyography (EMG) signals to classify and recognize the letters of the Spanish alphabet as interpreted in Peruvian sign language. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition based on EMG signal classification, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses a system that classifies EMG signals to recognize letters of the Spanish alphabet in Peruvian sign language. It reports a success rate of 93.9%, indicating that experiments were conducted to evaluate the system's performance, which qualifies this work as empirical.

papers.title: Dynamic Indonesian sign language recognition by using weighted K-Nearest Neighbor
papers.abstract: #excerpt: :LMD\DQWL 1XUXO .KRWLPDK 1DQLN 6XFLDWL <DK\D (ND 1XJ\DVD 5RPDULR :LMD\D ,QIRUPDWLFV 'HSDUWPHQW ,QVWLWXW 7HNQRORJL 6HSXOXK 1RSHPEHU 6XUDED\D HPDLO ZLMD\DQWL#LI LWV DF LG QDQLN#LI LWV DF LG Q \DK\DHND#JPDLO FRP ZLMD\D URPDULR#JPDLO FRP $EVWUDFW -3HRSOH ZLWK GLVDELOLW\ LQ ,QGRQHVLD LV RI WKH WRWDO SRSXODWLRQ $QG SHRSOH ZLWK KHDULQJ GLVDELOLW\ DUH WKH VHFRQG ODUJHVW 8VXDOO\ WKH\ KDYH D SUREOHP ZLWK FRPPXQLFDWLRQ $ VLJQ ODQJXDJH LV FUHDWHG WR VROYH WKH SUREOHP +RZHYHU LW LV OHVV SRSXODU VR RQO\ OLPLWHG SHRSOH XQGHUVWDQG LW 7KHUHIRUH D V\VWHP ZKLFK DEOH WR UHFRJQLVH VLJQ ODQJXDJH LV UHTXLUHG 7KLV UHVHDUFK LQWURGXFHV G\QDPLF IHDWXUH H[WUDFWLRQ WR UHFRJQLVH VLJQ ODQJXDJH '\QDPLF IHDWXUHV ZHUH XVHG WR FDSWXUH WUDMHFWRU\ PRYHPHQW RI KDQG VNHOHWRQ %HFDXVH VLJQ ODQJXDJHV DUH FKDUDFWHUL]HG QRW RQO\ E\ KDQG PRYHPHQW EXW DOVR E\ KDQG SRVLWLRQ EHVLGHV G\QDPLF IHDWXUHV WKLV UHVHDUFK DOVR XVHG KDQG SRVLWLRQ IHDWXUH )RU FODVVLILFDWLRQ WKLV VWXG\ XVHG D FRPELQDWLRQ RI :HLJKWHG 6LPSOH 0DWFKLQJ &RHII

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a method for recognizing Indonesian sign language using a weighted K-Nearest Neighbor algorithm. The primary contribution is a computational method specifically aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the development of a system for recognizing sign language using a weighted K-Nearest Neighbor approach, indicating hands-on experimental work. It implies the use of dynamic features for recognition, which suggests that the authors have implemented and tested their method, making this empirical research.

papers.title: Generative Sign-Description Prompts with Multi-Positive Contrastive Learning for Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: While sign language combines sequential hand motions with concurrent non-manual cues (e.g., mouth shapes and head tilts), current recognition systems lack multimodal annotation methods capable of capturing their hierarchical semantics. To bridge this gap, we propose GSP-MC, the first method integrating generative large language models into sign language recognition. It leverages retrieval-augmented generation with domain-specific large language models and expert-validated corpora to produce precise multipart descriptions. A dual-encoder architecture bidirectionally aligns hierarchical skeleton features with multi-level text descriptions (global, synonym, part) through probabilistic matching. The approach combines global and part-level losses with KL divergence optimization, ensuring robust alignment across relevant text-skeleton pairs while capturing sign semantics and detailed dynamics. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving 97.1% accuracy on the Chinese SLR500 (surpassing SSRL’s 96.9%) and 97.07% on the Turkish AUTSL (exceeding SML’s 96.85%), confirming cross-lingual potential for inclusive communication technologies.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method (GSP-MC) that integrates generative large language models into sign language recognition, addressing the multimodal aspects of sign language. The primary contribution is a method that enhances sign language recognition through advanced alignment techniques and achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a method that integrates generative large language models into sign language recognition and reports experiments demonstrating state-of-the-art performance with specific accuracy metrics on two datasets. The mention of surpassing previous methods and achieving high accuracy indicates empirical evaluation and comparison, making this work clearly empirical.

papers.title: Gesture to Speech: A Wearable Solution for Sign Language Translation
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a groundbreaking wearable device designed to address communication challenges faced by the global mute community. The device utilizes embedded flex sensors strategically placed on a glove to capture intricate sign language gestures. An embedded system, featuring a microcontroller (Arduino), processes these gestures through a sophisticated translation algorithm, converting them into both textual and speech output. The seamless integration of Bluetooth technology enables real-time wireless communication with external devices, such as smartphones or tablets. The device's versatile design ensures comfort and unobtrusiveness, making it suitable for various social, educational, and professional settings. Additionally, a rechargeable battery and efficient power management contribute to prolonged and sustainable usage. The embedded system's role is pivotal, acting as the cognitive hub that interprets and translates sign language gestures, fostering inclusivity and breaking down communication barriers. The translation algorithm, backed by machine learning techniques, enhances accuracy, making the device a reliable and practical solution. The optional inclusion of Text-to-Speech software further enriches the user experience, offering a comprehensive communication tool for the mute community. This paper discusses the device's technological architecture, design considerations, and real-world applications, emphasizing its transformative impact on global inclusivity. The research explores the potential for widespread adoption and its contribution to creating a more accessible and cohesive world.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a wearable device that captures sign language gestures using flex sensors and processes them through a translation algorithm to convert them into speech and text. The primary contribution is a computational method (the translation algorithm) that directly targets sign language, making it relevant to the scope of sign language processing.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the development of a wearable device that captures sign language gestures using flex sensors and processes them through a translation algorithm. It mentions the use of machine learning techniques to enhance accuracy, indicating that there is an experimental component involved in the implementation of the device. This suggests hands-on work with a system designed for real-world applications, making it empirical.

papers.title: Deep Learning-Based Sign Language Communication System with Multi-Language Support
papers.abstract: Speech impairment is regarded as one of the significant disabilities. When it comes to communicating with others, people with this limitation employ sign language. However, individuals with speech disabilities are unable to communicate with those who do not understand sign language. Therefore, our initiative aims to bridge this communication gap. The primary goal of this work is to develop a vision-based system that can recognise sign language motions in real-time and provide translation in multiple languages. We trained a CNN model on both spatial and temporal features present in real-time video sequences, achieving an accuracy of 97.5%. For multilingual translation, we employed the T5 transformer, achieving an average BLEU score of 0.193, ROUGE-L score of 0.038, and 0.224 seconds per sentence for English-to-Hindi, and a BLEU score of 0.048, ROUGE-L score of 0.022, and 0.434 seconds per sentence for English-to-Kannada translation.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a vision-based system that recognizes sign language motions in real-time and translates them into multiple languages using a CNN model and a T5 transformer. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition and translation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the training of a CNN model on real-time video sequences for sign language recognition, reporting an accuracy of 97.5%. It also discusses the use of the T5 transformer for multilingual translation, providing performance metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE-L scores. This indicates hands-on experimental work with clear quantitative findings, making it empirical.

papers.title: A Performance Comparison of Japanese Sign Language Recognition with ViT and CNN Using Angular Features
papers.abstract: In recent years, developments in deep learning technology have driven significant advancements in research aimed at facilitating communication with individuals who have hearing impairments. The focus has been on enhancing automatic recognition and translation systems for sign language. This study proposes a novel approach using a vision transformer (ViT) for recognizing Japanese Sign Language. Our method employs a pose estimation library, MediaPipe, to extract the positional coordinates of each finger joint within video frames and generate one-dimensional angular feature data from these coordinates. Then, the code arranges these feature data in a temporal sequence to form a two-dimensional input vector for the ViT model. To determine the optimal configuration, this study evaluated recognition accuracy by manipulating the number of encoder layers within the ViT model and compared against traditional convolutional neural network (CNN) models to evaluate its effectiveness. The experimental results showed 99.7% accuracy for the method using the ViT model and 99.3% for the results using the CNN. We demonstrated the efficacy of our approach through real-time recognition experiments using Japanese sign language videos.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel approach for recognizing Japanese Sign Language using a vision transformer (ViT) and compares it with traditional CNN models. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a hands-on experimental study that involves the implementation of a vision transformer (ViT) model for recognizing Japanese Sign Language. It reports on the evaluation of recognition accuracy, comparing the ViT model against traditional CNN models, and provides specific accuracy results of 99.7% and 99.3%. This indicates that the work is empirical as it includes experiments and performance metrics.

papers.title: Impact of Pose Estimation Models for Landmark-based Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Sign Language Recognition (SLR) models rely heavily on advances reached by Human Action Recognition (HAR). One of the simplest and most dimensionalreduced modalities is the skeleton joints and limbs represented with key-point landmarks and edges connecting these landmarks. These skeletons can be obtained by pose estimation, depth maps, or motion capture. For HAR, models are usually interested in less granularity of pose estimation compared to SLR, where the landmark estimation of not only the pose and body but the facial gestures, hands, and fingers is crucial. In this work, we compare three whole-body estimation libraries/models that are gaining attraction in the SLR task. We first find their relation by identifying common keypoints in their landmark structure and analyzing their quality. Then, we complement this analysis by comparing their annotations in three sign language datasets with videos of different quality, backgrounds, and regions (Peru and USA). Finally, we test a sign language recognition model to compare the quality of the annotations provided by these libraries/models.

filter_explanations.area: This paper evaluates the impact of various pose estimation models on landmark-based sign language recognition, which is a computational method aimed at improving SLR systems. The primary contribution involves analyzing and comparing the performance of these models in the context of sign language, thus fitting the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a comparative analysis of pose estimation models and their impact on sign language recognition. It mentions testing a sign language recognition model and comparing the quality of annotations from different libraries, which indicates hands-on experimental work. The analysis involves evaluating datasets and models, making this an empirical study.

papers.title: Sign Language Translator using Machine Learning Algorithms on RGB Color Space
papers.abstract: Automatic recognition and translation of American Sign Language (ASL) has become an important area of research in computer vision, with the aim of improving accessibility for Deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals. Despite recent progress, practical deployment remains challenging due to changing lighting conditions, background variations, and the computational demands of real-time processing. This work presents the development and experimental evaluation of a real-time ASL recognition system based on a YOLOv8m object detection framework trained using RGB color space images. The system was trained and tested on a dataset of static ASL hand gestures, including alphabetic characters, numeric digits, and selected symbolic gestures. Performance was assessed using standard detection metrics such as precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP). Experimental evaluation using live webcam input demonstrates consistent recognition performance under typical indoor lighting while supporting real-time operation. The results suggest that RGB-based deep learning object detection approaches can offer a practical and reliable solution for real-time ASL gesture recognition in assistive communication systems.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a machine learning-based system for the automatic recognition and translation of American Sign Language (ASL) using a YOLOv8m object detection framework. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and experimental evaluation of a real-time ASL recognition system using a YOLOv8m framework. It mentions training on a dataset of ASL hand gestures and assessing performance with standard detection metrics like precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP). This indicates hands-on experimental work, making it empirical.

papers.title: Towards Sign Language Motion Generation from Blurred Videos: System Development and Evaluation
papers.abstract: There has been growing interest in the automatic generation of sign language avatars from spoken language, with the aim of improving information accessibility for d/Deaf community. These approaches typically rely on three-dimensional motion capture data collected using specialized equipment and environments to generate sign language avatars. In contrast, the use of pose estimation techniques based on deep learning enables the extraction of sign language motion from monocular RGB video sources. This approach makes it possible to collect data from a wide range of sources, including broadcast video and webcam recordings. However, sign language videos are often affected by motion blur due to the rapid movements of hands and fingers. When pose estimation is applied to such videos, the output frequently contains temporally inconsistent hand shapes and sizes. This study presents a prototype system that automatically generates temporally consistent sign language motion from blurred videos. We also report the results of a user evaluation assessing the recognition rate and naturalness of the generated sign language motions. The results indicate that the difference in recognition rate between motion sequences generated from videos with and without blur was only 6.4%, indicating minimal impact. However, when motion blur occurred in keyframes—those crucial for interpreting the sign—both recognition rate and naturalness noticeably decreased.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system for generating sign language motion from blurred videos using pose estimation techniques. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving the generation of sign language motions, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language production/synthesis.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a prototype system for generating sign language motion from blurred videos and reports on a user evaluation assessing recognition rates and naturalness. The mention of results from the user evaluation, including specific findings about recognition rates and the impact of motion blur, indicates that this work is empirical as it involves testing and evaluation of a system.

papers.title: Design of SIBI Sign Language Recognition Using Artificial Neural Network Backpropagation
papers.abstract: Humans communicate with one another in the form of language. However, in social life, not all humans, as well as deaf people, can communicate verbally well. They tend to communicate with others using sign language. One of the sign language systems in Indonesia is SIBI. In its application, there are many obstacles when deaf or speech-impaired people communicate with normal people. This happens because of the lack of understanding of sign language in people who do not have special needs. The existence of a sign language translator system can overcome these obstacles. The development of this system utilizes the Neural Network Backpropagation algorithm as its classification algorithm. This research aims to find the best combination of its parameters based on the obtained accuracy.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a sign language recognition system specifically for the SIBI sign language using a Neural Network Backpropagation algorithm. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the development of a sign language recognition system using the Neural Network Backpropagation algorithm, indicating that the research involves hands-on experimental work. It also mentions the goal of finding the best combination of parameters based on accuracy, which implies an evaluation of the model's performance. This suggests that empirical methods are employed in the study.

papers.title: EasyTalk: A Translator for Sri Lankan Sign Language using Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
papers.abstract: Sign language is used by the hearing-impaired and inarticulate community to communicate with each other. But not all Sri Lankans are aware of the sign language or verbal languages and a translation is required. The Sri Lankan Sign Language is tightly bound to the hearing-impaired and inarticulate. The paper presents EasyTalk, a sign language translator which can translate Sri Lankan Sign Language into text and audio formats as well as translate verbal language into Sri Lankan Sign Language which would benefit them to express their ideas. This is handled in four separate components. The first component, Hand Gesture Detector captures hand signs using pre-trained models. Image Classifier component classifies and translates the detected hand signs. The Text and Voice Generator component produces a text or an audio formatted output for identified hand signs. Finally, Text to Sign Converter works on converting an entered English text back into the sign language based animated images. By using these techniques, EasyTalk can detect, translate and produce relevant outputs with superior accuracy. This can result in effective and efficient communication between the community with differently-abled people and the community with normal people.

filter_explanations.area: The paper presents EasyTalk, a comprehensive sign language translation system that utilizes machine learning and artificial intelligence to translate between Sri Lankan Sign Language and text/audio formats. The primary contribution is a computational method that involves multiple components for sign language recognition, translation, and production, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of EasyTalk, a sign language translator that involves multiple components, including a Hand Gesture Detector that uses pre-trained models, an Image Classifier for translating hand signs, and a Text to Sign Converter. The mention of using pre-trained models and the various components indicates hands-on experimental work, making this an empirical study focused on building and implementing a system.

papers.title: SignBERT: A BERT-based Deep Learning Framework for Continuous Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) is a very challenging task in intelligent systems, since it requires to produce real-time responses while performing computationally intensive video analytics and language modeling. Previous studies mainly focus on adopting hidden Markov models or recurrent neural networks with a limited capability to model specific sign languages, and the accuracy can drop significantly when recognizing the signs performed by different signers with non-standard gestures or non-uniform speeds. In this work, we develop a deep learning framework named SignBERT, integrating the bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) with the residual neural network (ResNet), to model the underlying sign languages and extract spatial features for CSLR. We further propose a multimodal version of SignBERT, which combines the input of hand images with an intelligent feature alignment, to minimize the distance between the probability distributions of the recognition results generated by the BERT model and the hand images. Experimental results indicate that when compared to the performance of alternative approaches for CSLR, our method has better accuracy with significantly lower word error rate on three challenging continuous sign language datasets.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents SignBERT, a deep learning framework specifically designed for continuous sign language recognition. The primary contribution is a computational method that integrates BERT with ResNet to improve the accuracy of recognizing sign languages, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a deep learning framework called SignBERT, which integrates BERT and ResNet for continuous sign language recognition. It mentions experimental results that indicate better accuracy and lower word error rates compared to alternative approaches, which implies that experiments were conducted to evaluate the model's performance on multiple datasets. This clearly supports the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: Smart IoT System for Indian Sign Language Translator
papers.abstract: This research study intends to transform communication for individuals with speech impairments by developing an innovative Indian Sign Language (ISL) to speech converter. The proposed wearable glove-based system empowers users to express themselves verbally, bridging the gap between sign language and spoken language. The glove is embedded with pushbuttons connected to an Arduino Uno microcontroller, which detects and interprets hand gestures. The Arduino UNO generates text corresponding to the detected movements, leveraging its programming capabilities. The generated text is then fed into a Python-based text-to-speech (TTS) system, which synthesizes natural-sounding speech. This integrated approach enables seamless conversion of ISL gestures into spoken language, facilitating effective communication. The project's successful implementation demonstrates the potential to transform the lives of individuals with speech disabilities, enhancing their social interactions, participation, and overall well-being. The ISL to Speech Converter project showcases the power of combining cutting-edge technologies like Arduino and Python to address real-world challenges, promoting inclusivity and accessibility. By providing a user-friendly and wearable solution, we aim to empower individuals with speech impairments to communicate confidently and independently, breaking down barriers and fostering a more inclusive society.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a wearable glove-based system that translates Indian Sign Language gestures into spoken language using an Arduino microcontroller and a text-to-speech system. The primary contribution is a computational method that processes sign language input to produce speech output, fitting the criteria for sign language production/synthesis.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a wearable glove-based system that detects and interprets hand gestures to convert Indian Sign Language into speech. This involves hands-on implementation using an Arduino microcontroller and a Python-based text-to-speech system, indicating experimental work. The successful implementation of this system demonstrates empirical research focused on addressing real-world challenges.

papers.title: Automatic Time-aware Recognition of Brazilian Sign Language Based on Dynamic Time Warping
papers.abstract: The Brazilian Sign Language (Libras) is a crucial communication medium for the deaf community in Brazil, yet it poses significant challenges for recognition and translation tasks. This paper presents a novel approach using Fast Dynamic Time Warping (FastDTW) for recognizing Libras signs in video streams. This approach aims to bridge the communication gap between deaf and hearing individuals, enhancing accessibility and reducing social marginalization. The methodology leverages MediaPipe to extract key hand and body landmarks, which are then used to compute angular features for accurate sign recognition. Experiments were conducted on the MINDS-Libras dataset, and the results demonstrated a high recognition accuracy, outperforming traditional methods. Furthermore, when the proposed model is applied to the INCLUDE-50 dataset containing signs from a different sign language, it performs competitively without relying on deep learning techniques.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for recognizing Brazilian Sign Language (Libras) using Fast Dynamic Time Warping, which is a specific algorithm for sign language recognition. The primary contribution is the development of a recognition system that processes video streams to identify signs, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a novel approach using Fast Dynamic Time Warping for recognizing Brazilian Sign Language signs, indicating that experiments were conducted on the MINDS-Libras dataset. It also mentions achieving high recognition accuracy and outperforming traditional methods, which implies empirical evaluation and comparison. The use of datasets for training and evaluation further supports the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: Prediction of Turkish Sign Language Alphabets Utilizing Deep Learning Method
papers.abstract: Sign Language (SL) is an important tool for communicating the hearing-impaired persons. Therefore, many sign languages exist depending on the language of the country. This study proposes using Turkish sign language (TSL) alphabets to exploit the evolution of deep learning (DL) science to convert mimes of sign language to text. Therefore, a new dataset is created with novelty in the type and conditions. The created dataset includes different conditions in backgrounds, lighting, and positions. Then, this paper suggests Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) models detect and classify the TSL alphabets signs. Moreover, the research tries to apply different hyperparameters to the developed models to get the best result. The best model of CNN contains five convolutional layers and uses the Adam learning rate method to adjust the accuracy. This model gives 97.80% accuracy after 70 epochs. Consequently, the suggested models with the challenging dataset are an important point for developing the sign language recognition field.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning method for recognizing Turkish Sign Language alphabets using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). It also introduces a new dataset that includes various conditions for training and evaluating the model. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the creation of a new dataset for Turkish sign language alphabets and the application of CNN models to detect and classify these signs. It mentions achieving 97.80% accuracy after training, indicating empirical evaluation of the model's performance. The work involves hands-on experimental methods, making it empirical.

papers.title: Determining the Effectiveness of the Developed Prototype That Translate Pakistan Sign Language into English text and speech.
papers.abstract: #tldr: Evaluating the effectiveness of the developed prototype, which translates Pakistan sign language into text and speech while using convolutional neural networking revealed that the tool proved to be effective in overcoming the communication barrier between deaf learners and unimpaired teachers.

filter_explanations.area: This paper discusses a prototype that translates Pakistan Sign Language into English text and speech, which involves a computational method for sign language translation. The primary contribution is the development and evaluation of a system that processes sign language, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract mentions evaluating the effectiveness of a developed prototype that translates Pakistan sign language into text and speech, indicating that experiments were conducted to assess its performance. The use of convolutional neural networking suggests that a model was implemented and tested, which qualifies this work as empirical.

papers.title: Deep Learning for Inclusive Communication: A CNN-Based Approach to Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Despite a wide social group that could profit from it, the idea of sign language recognition by technology is underutilized. Several technologies are available that can be helpful in establishing a link between this social group and the rest of the world. One of the main tools for enabling sign language users to communicate with the rest of society is understanding sign language. Computers can recognize sign language with the use of image categorization and machine learning, which can then be translated by humans. This study uses convolutional neural networks to identify sign language motions. The static sign language gestures were photographed using an RGB camera and comprise the image dataset used. The photos underwent preprocessing before being used as the input that had been cleaned. This dataset of sign language motions was retrained and tested using convolutional neural network model, the results of which are discussed in this study. The model has a number of convolution filter inputs that operate on a single input. Its ensuing validation accuracy was above 90%. In this work, the many attempts to detect sign language based on machine learning and image depth information are also examined. It assesses the different difficulties involved in solving the issue at hand and also describes the problem's potential future.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a convolutional neural network (CNN) approach specifically designed for recognizing static sign language gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the use of convolutional neural networks to identify sign language motions, indicating that a model was trained and tested on a dataset of images. It mentions preprocessing of the dataset and reports a validation accuracy above 90%, which are clear indicators of empirical work involving experiments and evaluations.

papers.title: Enhancing Sign Language Recognition Using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for Improved Accuracy in Indonesian Sign Language
papers.abstract: This research paper presents a sign language recognition system for Indonesian sign language, mainly for Bahasa Isyarat Indonesia (BISINDO) using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with the MobileNetV2 architecture. The system is developed to be able to recognize the BISINDO alphabet in real time using a camera input. The researcher is using a dataset consisting of 312 images that represents the BISINDO alphabet. The images are used for training and evaluation. The model achieved a validation accuracy of 98.08% and an $F 1$-score of 97.75%, indicating an impressive performance. This study demonstrated the potential of CNN and machine learning to help improve communication between hearing and speech impaired with non-disabled individuals.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for sign language recognition specifically targeting Indonesian Sign Language (BISINDO) using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The primary contribution is the development of a recognition system that processes sign language input and evaluates its performance, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a sign language recognition system using a CNN with the MobileNetV2 architecture. It mentions the use of a dataset of 312 images for training and evaluation, and reports validation accuracy and F1-score metrics, which are clear indicators of empirical work.

papers.title: Decomposing spontaneous sign language into elementary movements: A principal component analysis-based approach
papers.abstract: Sign Language (SL) is a continuous and complex stream of multiple body movement features. That raises the challenging issue of providing efficient computational models for the description and analysis of these movements. In the present paper, we used Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to decompose SL motion into elementary movements called principal movements (PMs). PCA was applied to the upper-body motion capture data of six different signers freely producing discourses in French Sign Language. Common PMs were extracted from the whole dataset containing all signers, while individual PMs were extracted separately from the data of individual signers. This study provides three main findings: (1) although the data were not synchronized in time across signers and discourses, the first eight common PMs contained 94.6% of the variance of the movements; (2) the number of PMs that represented 94.6% of the variance was nearly the same for individual as for common PMs; (3) the PM subspaces were highly similar across signers. These results suggest that upper-body motion in unconstrained continuous SL discourses can be described through the dynamic combination of a reduced number of elementary movements. This opens up promising perspectives toward providing efficient automatic SL processing tools based on heavy mocap datasets, in particular for automatic recognition and generation.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to analyze and decompose sign language movements into elementary movements. The findings suggest potential applications for automatic sign language processing tools, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria of developing computational methods for sign language recognition or generation.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper applies Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to motion capture data from signers, which involves hands-on data analysis and the extraction of principal movements. It reports findings related to the variance explained by the common and individual principal movements, indicating an empirical evaluation of the data. The use of a dataset and the analysis of its features suggest that this work is experimental in nature.

papers.title: Human Pose-Estimation and low-cost Interpolation for Text to Indian Sign Language
papers.abstract: Throughout India, the deaf community comprising millions of people uses Indian Sign Language. However, India houses only about 300 certified interpreters causing a lack of trained teachers and teaching materials in the country. This creates a language barrier with no means for translation without an interpreter. Previous approaches for creating a translation medium between spoken languages and sign languages are heavily dependent on Motion Capture technologies which are extremely expensive and not ideal for use by non-professionals even though highly accurate. Thus, computer vision approaches can be adopted, however, such approaches are highly dependent on huge labeled datasets. Datasets for sign language are hard to acquire and even more so for Indian Sign Language which has received little to no predominant attention in the past years. Based on that, this paper aims at generating 2D pose data of Indian signers from existing single-camera RGB videos. However, the videos are expected to be of varying quality, made using differently positioned cameras and consists of multiple signers against unified single-signer datasets. Unlike other applications, such as gait analysis, activity recognition, for sign-language face and hand key points play a major role. This paper represents the sub-project which is planned to be implemented as the preliminary stage to an actual ISL translation medium. In this paper, video is initially stabilized to reduce jitters and correct the perspective errors in videos. Then, 2D human-pose key points from the video are extracted and saved. Further, this saved data is analyzed and solved for missing key points which arise due to occlusions inherent in signs. For this, a low-cost trajectory interpolation method is used to fill the missing joint points. Overall, this vision-based motion capture approach gives a fairly non-distorted 2D pose model that is further corrected by using the information from neighboring frames simultaneously reducing time and computational cost. The resulting dataset serves as the base for further phases and research work.

filter_explanations.area: This paper focuses on generating 2D pose data for Indian Sign Language from RGB videos, which is a computational method aimed at improving the translation medium for sign language. The primary contribution involves the extraction and interpolation of pose key points, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria of developing resources for sign language processing systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper discusses generating 2D pose data from videos and involves the extraction and analysis of human-pose key points, which indicates hands-on experimental work. It also mentions the creation of a dataset that will serve as a foundation for further research, suggesting an empirical approach to developing a translation medium for Indian Sign Language.

papers.title: Application for the Recognition of Peruvian Sign Language using a Recurrent Neural Network
papers.abstract: Research into effective communication between individuals with hearing disabilities and listeners remains a significant challenge that requires innovative technological solutions. This study focuses on the development of a mobile application leveraging a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), specifically a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model, to facilitate smooth communication of the Peruvian sign language. Initial experiments yielded promising results, with the system achieving 98% accuracy and a 3-second response time. Tests were conducted with 40 users, divided between those proficient in sign language and those unfamiliar with it. However, due to the lack of an existing Peruvian Sign Language dataset, a new dataset was created from scratch, consisting of 15 distinct gestures. Future iterations will need to address this limitation to optimize performance with a larger dataset.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a mobile application that utilizes a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to recognize Peruvian Sign Language, which is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition. Additionally, it contributes a new dataset of gestures for training the system, aligning with the IN SCOPE criteria for both computational methods and new datasets.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a mobile application that utilizes an LSTM model for recognizing Peruvian sign language. It reports initial experiments with a 98% accuracy and mentions the creation of a new dataset consisting of 15 gestures, indicating hands-on experimental work and evaluation. This clearly qualifies the paper as empirical.

papers.title: TuniSL2025: A High-Quality Benchmark Dataset for Tunisian Sign Language Alphabet and Word Recognition
papers.abstract: We introduce Tunisian Sign Language, a comprehensive and fully annotated image dataset specifically designed for TSL recognition. This dataset aims to address the critical lack of high-quality resources available for regional Arabic sign languages, particularly in North Africa. TSL consists of 102 122 JPG images, captured from four different participants performing 32 Arabic alphabet signs and 82 commonly used Tunisian words. The images were collected using a high-resolution smartphone camera from three different angles (front, left, and right) to reflect real-world variability in perspective, lighting conditions, and backgrounds. To ensure consistency and data quality, preprocessing steps such as resizing, noise reduction, and image normalization were applied to all samples. The dataset includes both static hand gestures (for alphabet signs) and body-involved gestures (for vocabulary words), providing a rich and diverse visual representation of TSL. Each sign was performed by at least two different individuals, enhancing gesture diversity and making the dataset suitable for training robust machine learning and deep learning models. TSL is intended to serve as a benchmark for researchers and developers working on sign language recognition, computer vision, and assistive technologies. By making this dataset publicly available, we aim to promote inclusive AI development and facilitate communication accessibility for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community across the Arabic-speaking world.

filter_explanations.area: This paper introduces a comprehensive and fully annotated dataset specifically designed for Tunisian Sign Language recognition, which is intended for training and evaluating sign language recognition systems. The primary contribution is a new dataset that addresses the lack of resources for regional Arabic sign languages, fitting the IN SCOPE criterion for new datasets intended for training or evaluating such systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper introduces a comprehensive dataset specifically designed for Tunisian Sign Language recognition, which is intended for training machine learning and deep learning models. The detailed description of the dataset, including the number of images, the variety of signs, and the preprocessing steps, indicates that it is meant for empirical work in the field of sign language recognition.

papers.title: Indian Sign Language Interpretation using Skeletal Features and LSTM Networks
papers.abstract: Indian Sign Language (ISL) is a standardized and commonly used medium of communication for the deaf and dumb community in India. Despite an enormous deaf and dumb community, there are only about 300 certified ISL interpreters. This necessitates robust automated sign language interpretation systems. For the classification of gestures, we develop LSTM and Bi-LSTM networks trained on skeletal features extracted via MediaPipe. Augmentation techniques are used to enhance model performance. Furthermore, a uniform frame sampling method is applied to reduce computational complexity and overfitting. We achieve an accuracy of 97.92% with the proposed system, thus demonstrating its high usability.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for Indian Sign Language interpretation using LSTM and Bi-LSTM networks, focusing on gesture classification based on skeletal features. The primary contribution is a system designed for sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and training of LSTM and Bi-LSTM networks for gesture classification, indicating hands-on experimental work. It also mentions the use of augmentation techniques and a uniform frame sampling method, which are part of the model training process. The reported accuracy of 97.92% further supports that this is empirical work focused on evaluating the performance of the system.

papers.title: A Deep Learning Driven Flask Level Enhanced Web Application for Efficient Communication System
papers.abstract: Sign language is a crucial means of communication for millions worldwide but the effective learning of sign language is still hindered to this day. To overcome this, we propose a web application which makes use of neural networks and Mediapipe to create an E-Learning platform where users can learn, practice and assess their sign language skills. The main idea behind this initiative is that we can use the Mediapipe hands library to detect sign language gestures. Combining the power of neural networks with Mediapipe we can create an accurate way to detect sign language gestures. To further improve the efficiency and accuracy we also make use of a classifier. This classifier is used to create the Flask web application which serves as the sign language E-Learning platform. This platform comes equipped with resources for learning the sign language, interactive platform for practicing the sign language, and assessing your sign language skills. The combination of Mediapipe, Deep Learning and Flask together shows that new technologies can be used to reshape the E-learning industry and the platforms to be more inclusive and accessible.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a web application that utilizes deep learning and Mediapipe for detecting sign language gestures, which is a computational method aimed at improving sign language learning. The primary contribution is the integration of these technologies to create an E-Learning platform specifically for sign language, fitting the criteria for sign language recognition and educational resources.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a web application that utilizes neural networks and Mediapipe for detecting sign language gestures, indicating hands-on experimental work. The mention of a classifier and the integration of these technologies to create an E-Learning platform suggests that the authors are implementing and testing a system, which qualifies as empirical work.

papers.title: DEEP LEARNING MODEL FOR SIGN LANGUAGE INTERPRETATION USING WEB CAMERA
papers.abstract: #tldr: In the proposed work, the letters are detected from a video frame using convolutional neural network (CNN) and then converted into speech using Google Text-to-Speech (gTTS), and the systems are trained with 75% of images and tested with 25% of pictures from the database.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning model that utilizes a convolutional neural network (CNN) to interpret sign language from video frames and convert it into speech. The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a system that uses a CNN to detect letters from video frames and mentions training the system with a specific percentage of images, indicating hands-on experimental work. The use of a dataset for training and testing further supports that this is empirical work.

papers.title: Sign Language To Text Translator: A Semantic Approach With Ontological Framework
papers.abstract: This research paper presents a pioneering exploration into the development of a Sign Language to Text Translator (SLTT) that leverages an ontological framework. The study adopts a systematic approach, encompassing the definition of a structured knowledge representation, the mapping of sign language gestures to corresponding English text, and the implementation of a real-time translation system. The incorporation of an ontological framework provides a structured foundation, augmenting the accuracy and adaptability of the translator. The project seamlessly integrates both manual input simulation and potential machine learning models to enhance sign language recognition. The primary objective is to create an effective and versatile solution for mitigating communication barriers between the deaf and hearing communities.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for translating sign language into text using an ontological framework. The primary contribution is the development of a Sign Language to Text Translator, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language translation systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a real-time translation system for sign language to text, which indicates hands-on experimental work. It also mentions the integration of machine learning models to enhance recognition, suggesting that there is an empirical component to the research. The focus on developing a structured knowledge representation and mapping gestures to text implies a systematic approach that likely involves evaluation and testing.

papers.title: Sign to Text: Automated Sign Language Interpretation using LSTM and Computer Vision
papers.abstract: Sign language serves as a crucial communication tool for individuals with speech or hearing impairments. Recent advancements in computer vision and deep learning offer opportunities to develop efficient, real-time sign language recognition systems that bridge communication gaps. This study proposes a sign language recognition model utilizing the Mediapipe model and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to detect and interpret American Sign Language (ASL) gestures in real time. The proposed system extracts and analyzes key landmarks across the body, hands, and face, leveraging CNN-LSTM architecture to capture both spatial and temporal features essential for accurate gesture recognition. Evaluated on a diverse ASL dataset, the proposed model achieved an accuracy of 92%, demonstrating robustness and adaptability in varied environments. The proposed approach holds promise for applications in assistive technologies and educational tools, providing an accessible solution for sign language interpretation.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for sign language recognition using LSTM and computer vision techniques. The primary contribution is the development of a model that interprets American Sign Language gestures in real time, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a sign language recognition model using LSTM and CNN architectures, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions evaluation on a diverse ASL dataset and reports an accuracy of 92%, which supports the empirical nature of the study. The focus on real-time gesture recognition and the use of a specific dataset further emphasize the experimental aspect of the research.

papers.title: SEMANTICALLY ENHANCED MULTIMODAL NEURAL ARCHITECTURE FOR UZBEK SIGN LANGUAGE TRANSLATION
papers.abstract: This study proposes a semantically enriched multimodal neural architecture for automatic translation of Uzbek Sign Language (UZSL). In the approach, first, the text stream is brought to semantic consistency through n-gram tokenization, lemmatization, and morphological normalization, and a high-information context is prepared for BERT-MLM. Then, hand, face, and body landmarks are extracted from video frames using MediaPipe Holistic, speech transcriptions are generated using Speech-to-Text, and they are annotated with gloss dictionary-based labels in the text→gloss→landmark relationship. During the translation process, the text is fed to BERT-MLM, and semantic candidates are generated using a Top-k masking strategy; these candidates are checked against a standard gloss corpus in the constraint layer and filtered using semantic similarity calculations based on Word2Vec. Finally, a re-ranking mechanism based on SBERT evaluates the cosine similarity between the context and glosses and selects the most appropriate gloss. This integrated pipeline provides a near-real-time, consistent, and scalable translation workflow for UZSL by reducing the morphological complexity of the text, extracting stable features from multimodal signals, and selecting glosses based on semantic criteria with dictionary constraints.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a multimodal neural architecture specifically designed for the automatic translation of Uzbek Sign Language. The primary contribution is a computational method that integrates various techniques for processing and translating sign language, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language translation systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a comprehensive approach that involves extracting features from video frames, generating speech transcriptions, and utilizing a multimodal neural architecture for translation. The use of BERT-MLM and the evaluation of semantic candidates indicate that experiments and evaluations are conducted to assess the translation process. This hands-on work with a neural architecture and the integration of various modalities suggest that the study is empirical.

papers.title: Persian deaf sign language recognition system using deep learning
papers.abstract: Vision based communicating with compare of speech-based communicating is more complex and meaningful. Direct communication between deaf and other people is very difficult, so there are attempts for making a sign language interpreting system. Deep learning uses linear and nonlinear transformations to model the concepts in the data hierarchically. Deep learning also learns long time dependencies in sequential frames. A deep network has been created that has trained 14 words, and recognizes that words with its speed during playback. In this paper, data are given to two distinct systems and the results are compared with each other. The first system consists of three basic stages of hand and head tracking, feature extraction, and finally identification. Dynamic programming is used for tracking, that it is very timely. Right hand tracking is an important step. Possibility of placing two hands together causes the tracking system to be erroneous and occlusion. Dynamic algorithm, for hand tracking, has been explained. Difference frames has been used as score function and movement between two frame times has been used for penalty function in dynamic programming. Experiments from deaf news, has been tested, and well able to track right hand. The second system is a deep learning network with multiple layers that recognizes sign language word in a much higher speed than the first system.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning-based system for recognizing Persian sign language, detailing the architecture and methods used for hand and head tracking, feature extraction, and word identification. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the creation and training of a deep learning network to recognize sign language words, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions comparing results from two distinct systems, which implies an evaluation of performance. The use of dynamic programming for tracking and the mention of experiments further support that this is empirical work.

papers.title: A Machine Learning Based Sign Language Interpretation System for Communication with Deaf-mute People
papers.abstract: An utmost necessity of the deaf-mute people is to communicate with the non-mute people without the knowledge of sign language in different environments including office premises, shopping centers, educational institutions, and the like. Although several systems exist for teaching or learning sign language for the deaf-mute people, little attention has been paid to the development of useful and usable tools for effortless communication between mute and non-mute people. Therefore, the objective of this research is to develop an intelligent sign language interpretation system to connect with deaf-mute community, which will be used as a two way correspondence between speech impaired and regular speaking people. To attain this objective, firstly, a survey on recent vision-based gesture recognition approaches has been carried out. Secondly, an efficient and improved method for recognition of static hand posture and temporal gesture, focusing on finger-spelling method has been proposed which was developed using raspberry pi with the help of machine learning. Finally, the developed system was evaluated with 60 participants including the deaf-mute people and was found to be capable of successfully carrying out its functions with good performance.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a machine learning-based system for interpreting sign language, specifically focusing on recognizing static hand postures and temporal gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at facilitating communication between deaf-mute individuals and non-mute individuals, which aligns with the criterion of sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a sign language interpretation system that includes an evaluation with 60 participants, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions the use of machine learning for gesture recognition and reports that the system was found to perform well, which implies empirical evaluation and testing.

papers.title: ASL UNDERPRESSURE: Gamification of American Sign Language Learning Through Human-Computer Interaction
papers.abstract: ASL Underpressure is an innovative web-based game that utilizes video processing and deep learning models to improve the practice of American Sign Language (ASL) and bridge the communication gap between hearing and deaf communities. The game focuses on education and improving communication skills by challenging players to quickly and accurately form words using the ASL alphabet. The system incorporates a timer to maintain player engagement and allows for multiple attempts per word to encourage learning and mastery. This paper demonstrates the potential of technology to improve communication and foster understanding between diverse communities.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a web-based game that utilizes video processing and deep learning models to facilitate the learning of American Sign Language (ASL). The primary contribution involves a computational method aimed at enhancing sign language learning, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for educational tools that utilize sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract mentions the use of deep learning models and video processing in the context of a web-based game designed to improve ASL learning. While it does not provide specific experimental results or metrics, the incorporation of deep learning models implies hands-on work that likely involves training or implementing a system, which qualifies it as empirical.

papers.title: Bengali Sign language to text conversion using artificial neural network and support vector machine
papers.abstract: #excerpt: This paper presents a novel system that converts Bengali Sign language to text using an optimum system comprising of artificial neural networks and support vector machine (SVM). Microsoft Kinect is used to take the input, which is the hand sign performed in front of the camera. The captured hand sign is eventually recognized, after joint and wrist detection and by assessing the contours. Contour feature is extracted and is run through a SVM for classification of the sign. The contour finding algorithm utilizes the convex hull method, and the features extracted after detection is passed through the support vector model for recognition. To validate the performance of the proposed model, a dataset that consists of both male and female hand gesture images is utilized. Experimental results demonstrate 84.11% classification accuracy for our tested dataset. Keywords- Kinect, Hand Gesture, Neural Network, Bengali Sign, Classification Accuracy. Sign language is a form of communication that peop

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for converting Bengali Sign Language to text using artificial neural networks and support vector machines. The primary contribution is the development of a system that recognizes sign language gestures and translates them into text, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes a system that converts Bengali Sign language to text using artificial neural networks and support vector machines, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions the use of a dataset for validation and reports a classification accuracy of 84.11%, which supports its empirical nature.

papers.title: Interactive learning of natural sign language with radar
papers.abstract: Over the past decade, there have been great advancements in radio frequency sensor technology for human–computer interaction applications, such as gesture recognition, and human activity recognition more broadly. While there is a significant amount of study on these topics, in most cases, experimental data are acquired in controlled settings by directing participants what motion to articulate. However, especially for communicative motions, such as sign language, such directed data sets do not accurately capture natural, in situ articulations. This results in a difference in the distribution of directed American Sign Language (ASL) versus natural ASL, which severely degrades natural sign language recognition in real‐world scenarios. To overcome these challenges and acquire more representative data for training deep models, the authors develop an interactive gaming environment, ChessSIGN, which records video and radar data of participants as they play the game without any external direction. The authors investigate various ways of generating synthetic samples from directed ASL data, but show that ultimately such data does not offer much improvement over just initialising using imagery from ImageNet. In contrast, an interactive learning paradigm is proposed by the authors in which model training is shown to improve as more and more natural ASL samples are acquired and augmented via synthetic samples generated from a physics‐aware generative adversarial network. The authors show that the proposed approach enables the recognition of natural ASL in a real‐world setting, achieving an accuracy of 69% for 29 ASL signs—a 60% improvement over conventional training with directed ASL data.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel interactive learning paradigm for recognizing natural sign language using radar and video data. The primary contribution is a computational method that enhances sign language recognition by leveraging natural ASL samples and synthetic data, which fits the criterion of sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an interactive gaming environment for collecting natural ASL data and reports on the training of models using this data. It mentions achieving an accuracy of 69% for recognizing ASL signs and a significant improvement over conventional methods, indicating that experiments and evaluations were conducted. This clearly supports the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: S3DRGF: Spatial 3-D Relational Geometric Features for 3-D Sign Language Representation and Recognition
