papers.abstract: Locations, angles, edges, and surfaces are spatial joint features that were predominantly used for characterizing three-dimensional (3-D) skeletal data in human action recognition. Despite their demonstrated success, features described earlier find difficulty in representing a relational change among joint movements in 3-D space for classifying human actions. To characterize a relation between joints on 3-D skeleton, we propose spatial 3-D relational geometric features (S3DRGFs). S3DRGFs are calculated on a subset of four joints in a chronological order covering all joints on the skeleton. Each of these four joints shape into a polygon, that reshapes spatially and temporally with respect to the sign (action) in the 3-D video. Consequently, we construct the spatio-temporal features (S3DRGF) by computing the area and perimeter of these polygons. Accordingly, query 3-D sign (action) recognition process transforms the joint area and perimeter features (JAF and JPF) into global alignment kernels based on the computed similarity scores with the dataset features. The similarity scores from JAF and JPF kernels are averaged for recognition. The proposed framework has been tested on our own 3-D sign language dataset (BVC3DSL) and three other publicly available datasets: HDM05, CMU, and NTU RGBD skeletal data. The results show higher levels of accuracy in decoding 3-D sign language into text for building a 3-D model based sign language translator.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method (S3DRGF) for recognizing 3-D sign language by utilizing spatial relational geometric features derived from joint movements. The primary contribution is the development of a framework that processes 3-D skeletal data specifically for sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a new feature extraction method for 3-D sign language recognition and reports that the proposed framework has been tested on both a new dataset (BVC3DSL) and three existing datasets. It mentions achieving higher accuracy in decoding 3-D sign language, indicating empirical evaluation of the method's performance.

papers.title: Sign Lang age Recogni ion sing Openc and HSV color Model
papers.abstract: Communication is the process of exchanging information, views and expressions between two or more person. The two types of communication are verbal and nonverbal. Hand gestures are nonverbal method of communication. A more organized form of hand gestures is known as sign. The physically disabled person uses these gestures to communicate with everyone. To design a system that can recognize sign language accurately so that physically impaired person can have communication with normal people is the main aim for this paper. System follows the general steps like capturing the image from webcam, compare the image with stored images in data set and convert the gesture into text which is readable by disabled person. It is done by using HSV color model and Opencv libraries.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system for recognizing sign language gestures using image processing techniques with OpenCV and the HSV color model. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a system designed to recognize sign language by capturing images, comparing them with a dataset, and converting gestures into text. This indicates hands-on experimental work involving image processing and a dataset, which supports the conclusion that the paper is empirical.

papers.title: Sign Tone: A Deep Learning-Based Deaf Companion System for Two Way Communication Between Deaf and Non-Deaf Individuals
papers.abstract: Communication is essential to express and receive information, knowledge, ideas, and views among people, but it has been quite a while to be an obstruction for people with hearing and mute disabilities. Sign language is one method of communicating with deaf people. Though there is sign language to communicate with non-sign people it is difficult for everyone to interpret and understand. The performance of existing sign language recognition approaches is typically limited. Developing an assistive device that will translate the sign language to a readable format will help the deaf-mutes to communicate with ease to the common people. Recent advancements in the development of deep learning, deep neural networks, especially Temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) have provided solutions to the communication of deaf and mute individuals. In this project, the main objective is to design Deaf Companion System for that to develop SignNet Model to provide two-way communication of deaf individuals and to implement an automatic speaking system for deaf and mute people. It provides two-way communication for all classes of people (deaf-and-mute, hard of hearing, visually impaired, and non-signers) and can be scaled commercially. The proposed system, consists of three modules; the sign recognition module (SRM) that recognizes the signs of a deaf individual using TCN, the speech recognition using Hidden Marko Model and synthesis module (SRSM) that processes the speech of a non-deaf individual and converts it to text, and an Avatar module (AM) to generate and perform the corresponding sign of the non-deaf speech, which were integrated into the sign translation companion system called deaf companion system to facilitate the communication from the deaf to the hearing and vice versa. The proposed model is trained on Indian Sign Language. Then developed a web-based user interface to deploy SignNet Model for ease of use. Experimental results on MNIST sign language recognition datasets validate the superiority of the proposed framework. The TCN model gives an accuracy of 98.5%..

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a comprehensive system (Deaf Companion System) that includes a sign recognition module using deep learning (Temporal Convolutional Networks) specifically for Indian Sign Language. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at facilitating two-way communication between deaf and non-deaf individuals, which aligns with the criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a SignNet Model for a Deaf Companion System, which includes a sign recognition module that uses Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs). It mentions that the model is trained on Indian Sign Language and reports an accuracy of 98.5% on the MNIST sign language recognition datasets, indicating hands-on experimental work and evaluation of the system's performance.

papers.title: Enhancing American Sign Language Classification by Leveraging Hand Landmark Extraction
papers.abstract: This research explores a novel approach to enhance the accuracy of static American Sign Language (ASL) sign classification by employing hand landmark extraction through computer vision. Traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) often operate on raw pixel data, leading to high-dimensional feature spaces and extensive training times. In contrast, our method extracts key features as data points, significantly reducing the dimensionality of the data. We demonstrate that this hand landmark detection-based approach yields promising accuracy with shallower models such as k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Logistic Regression, due to the inherently lower dimensionality of the data and the elimination of background clutter. Importantly, our approach excels particularly when dealing with relatively small datasets, a critical advantage considering the limited availability of public data in this field. Our research highlights the efficiency and effectiveness of hand landmark extraction as a preprocessing technique for ASL sign classification using the Google MediaPipe hand landmark model.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel approach to enhance the classification of American Sign Language signs by utilizing hand landmark extraction as a preprocessing technique. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition accuracy, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses a novel approach to enhance ASL sign classification by employing hand landmark extraction and mentions that this method yields promising accuracy with various models, indicating an experimental evaluation. The use of hand landmark detection and the mention of model performance suggest that the research involves hands-on work and data-driven results, making it empirical.

papers.title: A Deep Learning Mobile Application based Sign Language Recognition for Aphasic Person
papers.abstract: Pervasive  sign language  is  used relatively within our society, deaf and other verbally challenged communication of people with hearing impairments is of prime importance in our society.  This  paper  presents  a  solution  for  this  purpose.  We developed an android application which integrates both computer  vision  techniques  involving  Histogram  of  Oriented Gradients  (HOG)  as  well  as  Machine  Learning  techniques such as Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Multiclass Support  Vector  Machine  (SVM)  to  detect  and  recognize gestures automatically. People tend to face difficulty in communicating on daily basis. Because of that development of assistive,  cost-effective,  as  well  as  non-invasive  technologies to aid the people in need is necessity.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a mobile application that utilizes computer vision and machine learning techniques (HOG, CNN, SVM) for the automatic recognition of sign language gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an android application that utilizes computer vision techniques and machine learning methods, specifically mentioning the use of CNN and SVM for gesture detection and recognition. This indicates hands-on experimental work in building and implementing a system for sign language recognition, making it empirical.

papers.title: Recognition of Bangladeshi Sign Language (BdSL) Words using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs)
papers.abstract: In a world where effective communication is fundamental, individuals who are Deaf and Dumb (D&D) often face unique challenges due to their primary mode of communication—sign language. Despite the interpreters' invaluable roles, their lack of availability causes communication difficulties for the D&D individuals. This study explores whether the field of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) could be a potential solution. The primary objective is to assist D&D individuals with computer applications that could act as mediators to bridge the communication gap between them and the wider hearing population. To ensure their independent communication, we propose an automated system that could detect specific Bangla Sign Language (BdSL) words, addressing a critical gap in the sign language detection and recognition literature. Our approach leverages deep learning and transfer learning principles to convert webcam-captured hand gestures into textual representations in real-time. The model's development and assessment rest upon 992 images created by the authors, categorized into ten distinct classes representing various BdSL words. Our findings show the DenseNet201 and ResNet50-V2 models achieve promising training and testing accuracies of 99% and 93%, respectively. Doi: 10.28991/ESJ-2023-07-06-019 Full Text: PDF

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for recognizing Bangladeshi Sign Language (BdSL) words using deep convolutional neural networks. The primary contribution is the development of an automated system that detects specific sign language gestures and converts them into textual representations, which fits the criterion for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an automated system for recognizing Bangladeshi Sign Language words using deep learning techniques. It mentions the creation of a dataset of 992 images and reports training and testing accuracies of 99% and 93%, indicating empirical evaluation of the models. This hands-on experimental work clearly qualifies as empirical.

papers.title: Training CNNs for 3-D Sign Language Recognition With Color Texture Coded Joint Angular Displacement Maps
papers.abstract: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can be remarkably effective for recognizing two-dimensional and three-dimensional (3-D) actions. To further explore the potential of CNNs, we applied them in the recognition of 3-D motion-captured sign language (SL). The sign's 3-D spatio-temporal information of each sign was interpreted using joint angular displacement maps (JADMs), which encode the sign as a color texture image; JADMs were calculated for all joint pairs. Multiple CNN layers then capitalized on the differences between these images and identify discriminative spatio-temporal features. We then compared the performance of our proposed model against those of the state-of-the-art baseline models by using our own 3-D SL dataset and two other benchmark action datasets, namely, HDM05 and CMU.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a method for recognizing 3-D sign language using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and introduces a novel representation of sign language through joint angular displacement maps. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the application of CNNs for recognizing 3-D sign language, detailing the use of joint angular displacement maps and the comparison of the proposed model's performance against state-of-the-art baseline models. It also mentions the use of a custom 3-D sign language dataset and two benchmark datasets, indicating hands-on experimental work and evaluation, which qualifies this as empirical.

papers.title: SignAligner: Harmonizing Complementary Pose Modalities for Coherent Sign Language Generation
papers.abstract: Sign language generation aims to produce diverse sign representations based on spoken language. However, achieving realistic and naturalistic generation remains a significant challenge due to the complexity of sign language, which encompasses intricate hand gestures, facial expressions, and body movements. In this work, we introduce PHOENIX14T+, an extended version of the widely-used RWTH-PHOENIX-Weather 2014T dataset, featuring three new sign representations: Pose, Hamer and Smplerx. We also propose a novel method, SignAligner, for realistic sign language generation, consisting of three stages: text-driven pose modalities co-generation, online collaborative correction of multimodality, and realistic sign video synthesis. First, by incorporating text semantics, we design a joint sign language generator to simultaneously produce posture coordinates, gesture actions, and body movements. The text encoder, based on a Transformer architecture, extracts semantic features, while a cross-modal attention mechanism integrates these features to generate diverse sign language representations, ensuring accurate mapping and controlling the diversity of modal features. Next, online collaborative correction is introduced to refine the generated pose modalities using a dynamic loss weighting strategy and cross-modal attention, facilitating the complementarity of information across modalities, eliminating spatiotemporal conflicts, and ensuring semantic coherence and action consistency. Finally, the corrected pose modalities are fed into a pre-trained video generation network to produce high-fidelity sign language videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SignAligner significantly improves both the accuracy and expressiveness of the generated sign videos.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method, SignAligner, for generating realistic sign language representations from spoken language input. It also introduces an extended dataset (PHOENIX14T+) for training and evaluation, which aligns with the criteria for new datasets intended for sign language processing. The primary contribution is clearly focused on sign language generation, making it in scope.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the introduction of a new dataset (PHOENIX14T+) and a novel method (SignAligner) for sign language generation, which involves multiple stages of generation and correction. It mentions extensive experiments that demonstrate improvements in accuracy and expressiveness, indicating that empirical evaluations were conducted to assess the performance of the proposed method.

papers.title: Utilising Transformers for American Sign Language Fingerspelling Recognition
papers.abstract: This paper presents a machine learning approach to American Sign Language (ASL) fingerspelling recognition using Transformer models. Addressing the challenges of high variability in hand shapes, movement, and signing speed, the study utilises the new ASL Fingerspelling Recognition Corpus and the CRISP-DM methodology to develop and evaluate a proof-of-concept model. The model achieved a mean Levenshtein distance of 4.7, corresponding to an error rate of 16.37%. This research demonstrates the feasibility of using advanced AI techniques to enhance accessibility for the deaf and hard of hearing communities by translating ASL fingerspelling into text.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a machine learning approach specifically for recognizing American Sign Language fingerspelling using Transformer models. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, and it also utilizes a new dataset for training and evaluation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper develops and evaluates a proof-of-concept model for ASL fingerspelling recognition using Transformer models. It utilizes the ASL Fingerspelling Recognition Corpus for training and reports a mean Levenshtein distance and error rate, which are quantitative performance metrics. This indicates that the work is empirical as it involves hands-on experimental work and evaluation.

papers.title: Towards Real-time Sign Language Interpreting Robot: Evaluation of Non-manual Components on Recognition Accuracy
papers.abstract: #tldr: The purpose of this work is to develop a human-robot interaction system that could be used as a sign language interpreter and investigate whether non-manual components would improve sign’s recognition accuracy.

filter_explanations.area: This paper focuses on developing a human-robot interaction system aimed at sign language interpretation, which involves recognition of signs and potentially improving accuracy through non-manual components. The primary contribution relates to a computational method for sign language recognition, fitting within the scope of sign language processing.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract indicates that the work involves developing a human-robot interaction system for sign language interpretation and evaluates the impact of non-manual components on recognition accuracy. This suggests an experimental evaluation of the system's performance, which qualifies it as empirical work.

papers.title: An overview of searchings algorithm for Indonesian to German sign language statistical machine translation
papers.abstract: #excerpt: Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) is one of the research areas in computer science. The research of Statistical Machine Translation shows a momentous output in the denary of years. Primarily, the research focuses on how to translate from one language to another language and vice versa. It rarely discusses the searching process of Statistical Machine Translation. The objectives of this paper are how to analyst searching algorithm for Indonesian to German sign language Statistical Machine Translation. Searching algorithms are used in the Statistical Machine Translation including: binary, linear, and interpolation which is used to translate Indonesian to German sign language and vice versa. The translation process used combination of Markov chains and searching algorithms. Around 25.000 data (corpus data) translation were prepared to analyze time execution. The results of translation show the output of searching algorithms. Interpolation search algorithm gives the best time of transla

filter_explanations.area: This paper discusses the use of searching algorithms in the context of statistical machine translation between Indonesian and German sign language. The primary contribution involves computational methods related to translation, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language translation systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the use of searching algorithms in the context of Statistical Machine Translation, mentioning the preparation of a corpus of around 25,000 data points for analysis. It also reports on the performance of different algorithms, indicating empirical evaluation of their effectiveness in translation tasks. This suggests that the paper involves experimental work, making it empirical.

papers.title: Classification of Sign language Using VGG16 and VGG19
papers.abstract: The Translation System (TS) serves as a communication bridge for individuals with disabilities to interact with others in society. As computers play a vital role in our daily lives, advancements in human-computer interaction (HCI) have particularly benefited the deaf and mute community. Our proposed system aims to develop an intelligent translator that facilitates seamless communication between individuals with speech impairments and the general population. This system seeks to establish an effective and efficient means of communication for those with speaking deficiencies and regular individuals. The proposed system aims to convert sign language into written text using pre-trained models of VGG16 and VGG19, we used The Sign Language dataset consists of 27,455 training samples and 7,172 test samples, This model gave 97.5% with VGG16 and 96% with VGG19.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for classifying sign language using pre-trained models (VGG16 and VGG19) to convert sign language into written text. The primary contribution is a system that directly targets sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a sign language translation system that utilizes pre-trained VGG16 and VGG19 models. It mentions the use of a dataset with a substantial number of training and test samples, and it reports performance metrics of 97.5% and 96% accuracy for the respective models. This indicates hands-on experimental work and evaluation, making it empirical.

papers.title: Sign Language Recognition for Forensic Analysis Using CNN and Transfer Learning
papers.abstract: Sign language plays an important role in forensic procedures. This is significant in giving evidence and communicating of rights, especially during taking statements from a non-verbal victim or suspect. It ensures the authenticity of information from a deaf or mute individual so that there will be no confusion or omission whatsoever to affect the course of an investigation. This paper proposes a sign recognition approach that satisfies forensic needs by enabling the identification and interpretation of key gestures in crime scene recordings, surveillance footage, and other visual evidence. This proposed system will be leveraging the use of convolutional neural networks, which are empowered with the concept of transfer learning of MobileNetV2. By leveraging the training on American Sign Language, the model is able to differentiate between the different signs which might empower analysts' work in such segments. The best-performing model achieved an accuracy of 96%, along with high F1-score, precision, recall, and specificity, demonstrating robustness for forensic applications.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for sign language recognition using convolutional neural networks and transfer learning, specifically targeting forensic applications. The primary contribution is the development of a system that identifies and interprets sign language gestures, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a proposed sign recognition system that utilizes convolutional neural networks and transfer learning, indicating that a model is being trained and tested. It also mentions achieving an accuracy of 96% and high performance metrics such as F1-score, precision, recall, and specificity, which are clear indicators of empirical evaluation.

papers.title: Prototype of a Text to Ecuadorian Sign Language Translator Using a 3d Virtual Avatar
papers.abstract: Due to existing limitations in professional, educative, and social fields of deaf and hard of hearing people in Ecuador, a novel Sign Language Traducer System has been proposed in this work. It can be used to teach and translate phonemes or sentences to Ecuadorian Sign Language (LSEC by its acronym in Spanish) using a 3D virtual avatar called "Ema" that was designed using a software called "Makehuman". The database contains 133 sign animations that were elaborated using Blender. The graphic user interface (GUI) with a button for each sign, and a search bar were designed in Unity 3D. In relation to the text processing capabilities of the system, up to 60% of accuracy has been obtained using the Damerau-Levenshtein algorithm and an accuracy up to 95.66% has been obtained using the Jaro-Winkler algorithm, both of them have been coded in C# in the compiler Visual Studio Community. In this way, if the word entered in the search bar has a sign, the character will interpret it. Otherwise, the word will be spelled.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational system designed to translate text into Ecuadorian Sign Language using a 3D virtual avatar. The primary contribution is a method for sign language translation, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language translation systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a Sign Language Translator System that includes a database of 133 sign animations and reports accuracy metrics of 60% and 95.66% using specific algorithms. This indicates hands-on experimental work involving the implementation of a system and evaluation of its performance, making it empirical.

papers.title: 3D-LEX v1.0 – 3D Lexicons for American Sign Language and Sign Language of the Netherlands
papers.abstract: In this work, we present an efficient approach for capturing sign language in 3D, introduce the 3D-LEX v1.0 dataset, and detail a method for semi-automatic annotation of phonetic properties. Our procedure integrates three motion capture techniques encompassing high-resolution 3D poses, 3D handshapes, and depth-aware facial features, and attains an average sampling rate of one sign every 10 seconds. This includes the time for presenting a sign example, performing and recording the sign, and archiving the capture. The 3D-LEX dataset includes 1,000 signs from American Sign Language and an additional 1,000 signs from the Sign Language of the Netherlands. We showcase the dataset utility by presenting a simple method for generating handshape annotations directly from 3D-LEX. We produce handshape labels for 1,000 signs from American Sign Language and evaluate the labels in a sign recognition task. The labels enhance gloss recognition accuracy by 5% over using no handshape annotations, and by 1% over expert annotations. Our motion capture data supports in-depth analysis of sign features and facilitates the generation of 2D projections from any viewpoint. The 3D-LEX collection has been aligned with existing sign language benchmarks and linguistic resources, to support studies in 3D-aware sign language processing.

filter_explanations.area: This paper introduces the 3D-LEX v1.0 dataset, which includes 3D representations of signs from American Sign Language and the Sign Language of the Netherlands. It also details a method for semi-automatic annotation of phonetic properties and demonstrates the dataset's utility in improving recognition accuracy. The primary contribution is a new dataset intended for training and evaluating sign language processing systems, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper introduces the 3D-LEX v1.0 dataset, which includes 1,000 signs from American Sign Language and the Sign Language of the Netherlands. It also reports on the evaluation of handshape labels generated from this dataset, showing an improvement in gloss recognition accuracy by 5% over using no handshape annotations. This indicates hands-on experimental work and the use of a dataset for evaluation, making it empirical.

papers.title: Hand Posture Recognition Using Skeletal Data and Distance Descriptor
papers.abstract: In this paper, a method for the recognition of static hand postures based on skeletal data was presented. A novel descriptor was proposed. It encodes information about distances between particular hand points. Five different classifiers were tested, including four common methods and a proposed modification of nearest neighbor classifier, which can distinguish between posture classes differing mostly in hand orientation. The experiments were performed using three challenging datasets of gestures from Polish and American Sign Languages. The proposed method was compared with other approaches found in the literature. It outperforms every compared method, including our previous work, in terms of recognition rate.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a method for recognizing static hand postures using skeletal data, which is relevant to sign language recognition. The primary contribution is a computational method that utilizes a novel descriptor and classifiers to improve recognition rates for hand postures in Polish and American Sign Languages, fitting the criterion for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper presents a method for recognizing static hand postures and reports on experiments conducted with five different classifiers. It compares the proposed method against existing approaches and claims to outperform them in terms of recognition rate, indicating that empirical evaluation and data-driven work were performed.

papers.title: Evaluation of 3D Convolutional Neural Networks on Isolated Brazilian Sign Language Recognition
papers.abstract: Isolated Sign Language Recognition plays a crucial role in advancing technologies that enhance accessibility for deaf individuals. In this work we investigate the performance of several 3D Convolutional Neural Networks on the task of ISLR in LIBRAS. Models were trained on the MINDS dataset and evaluated on an out-of-domain dataset, MALTA-LIBRAS. Experimental results indicate that while all models achieve high metrics on the training and validation data, they exhibit clear overfitting and fail to generalize effectively to unseen samples. These results highlight the challenges posed by limited data and data variability in ISLR for LIBRAS.

filter_explanations.area: This paper investigates the performance of various 3D Convolutional Neural Networks specifically for the task of isolated Brazilian Sign Language recognition (ISLR). The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses the training and evaluation of several 3D Convolutional Neural Networks on the task of isolated Brazilian Sign Language recognition. It mentions the use of the MINDS dataset for training and an out-of-domain dataset for evaluation, along with reporting on the performance and challenges faced by the models, indicating empirical work.

papers.title: A Speech-driven Sign Language Avatar Animation System for Hearing Impaired Applications
papers.abstract: Sign language is the communication language used in hearing impaired community. Recently, the research of sign language production has made great progress but still need to cope with some critical challenges. In this paper, we propose a system-level scheme and push forward the implementation of sign language production for practical usage. We build a system capable of translating speech into sign language avatar. Different from previous approach only focusing on single technology, we systematically combine algorithms of language translation, body gesture animation and facial avatar generation. We also develop two applications: Sign Language Interpretation APP and Virtual Sign Language Anchor, to facilitate easy and clear communication for hearing impaired people.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system for producing sign language through an avatar based on speech input, which involves computational methods for language translation, gesture animation, and facial avatar generation. The primary contribution is a computational method that directly relates to sign language production, making it fit within the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a system that translates speech into sign language using a combination of algorithms for language translation, gesture animation, and avatar generation. This indicates hands-on experimental work and implementation of a system, which qualifies it as empirical.

papers.title: Development of an Arabic Sign Language Recognition System Utilizing Deep Convolutional Neural Network
papers.abstract: The ability to recognize sign language movements, especially Arabic Sign Language (ArSL), is necessary to increase the conversation accessibility for the those who are hard of hearing or deaf. However, there are a number of drawbacks to the current ASLR techniques, such as their dependency on manually created features, lack of datasets, and inefficiencies in real-time applications. This study suggests a novel method that utilize Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model designed especially for ASLR applications to overcome these difficulties. The suggested DCNN overcomes the drawbacks of conventional handmade feature-based techniques by automatically extracting discriminative features from unprocessed sign language images using hierarchical feature learning. Preprocessing techniques such as normalization and scaling are applied to improve the 7,857 completely labeled images in the RGB Arabic Alphabet Sign Language (AASL) the data set, which is provided for ASLR model evaluation and training. The results of the study demonstrate how successful the recommended DCNN model is, as evidenced by its astounding 99% accuracy on the AASL dataset. Comparison with similar approaches like VGG-16, ResNet-18, and other Efficient Net based architecture, shows the accuracy to which the proposed method can classify Arabic sign language alphabets. The findings presented evidence for how the chosen architecture for DCNNs can significantly enhance ASLR improvement, open up opportunities for improving communication interaction with Deaf and hard-of-hearing people and their integration. In doing the proposed work, we use Python.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) specifically designed for Arabic Sign Language Recognition (ASLR). The primary contribution is a computational method that addresses existing challenges in ASLR by automatically extracting features from sign language images and providing a dataset for evaluation and training. This aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and evaluation of a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model specifically for Arabic Sign Language Recognition. It mentions the use of a dataset with 7,857 labeled images for training and evaluation, reports an impressive accuracy of 99%, and compares the proposed method against other architectures like VGG-16 and ResNet-18. This indicates hands-on experimental work, making it empirical.

papers.title: Hysup-V: Quality-Aware 3D Vietnamese Sign Language Reconstruction With Anchor-Guided Kinematic Fusion
papers.abstract: Reconstructing expressive 3D signing motion from in-the-wild monocular video remains difficult due to motion blur, compression artifacts, frequent hand and face occlusions. These degradations make standard pipelines temporally unstable and prone to losing fine articulator detail. We introduce HySUP-V, a reliability-aware framework that assesses and manages visual evidence quality across sequences. In Stage I ($E Q A$), we score part-level image quality to select high-fidelity anchor frames as references. In Stage II (AKF), we enforce shape consistency by fusing specialized hand (MANO) and face (FLAME) estimates into a unified SMPL-X model and use geodesic pose transport to bridge degraded frames. Applying HySUP-V, we curate VSL-3D, a public dataset of 1,540 Vietnamese Sign Language clips with expressive SMPL-X parameters. Human evaluations yield a Mean Opinion Score of 3.47/5, plausible reconstructions with strong semantic preservation and hand accuracy, while highlighting remaining challenges in facial detail and motion naturalness.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents HySUP-V, a framework for reconstructing 3D Vietnamese Sign Language motion from video, which involves computational methods for assessing and managing visual evidence quality. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving the quality of sign language reconstruction, and it also introduces a new dataset (VSL-3D) for evaluating such systems. This fits the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods related to sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper introduces a framework for reconstructing 3D signing motion and reports on the creation of a public dataset of 1,540 Vietnamese Sign Language clips. It also discusses human evaluations and provides a Mean Opinion Score, indicating an experimental evaluation of the proposed method. This hands-on work with a dataset and evaluation metrics supports the empirical nature of the research.

papers.title: A Static Sign Language Recognition Method Enhanced with Self-Attention Mechanisms
papers.abstract: For the current wearable devices in the application of cross-diversified user groups, it is common to face the technical difficulties of static sign language recognition accuracy attenuation, weak anti-noise ability, and insufficient system robustness due to the differences in the use of users. This paper proposes a novel static sign language recognition method enhanced by a self-attention mechanism. The key features of sign language gesture classification are highlighted by the weight function, and then the self-attention mechanism is combined to pay more attention to the key features, and the convolutional neural network is used to extract the features and classify them, which realizes the accurate recognition of different types of static sign language under standard gestures and non-standard gestures. Experimental results reveal that the proposed method achieves an average accuracy of 99.52% in the standard static sign language recognition task when tested against the standard 36 static gestures selected within the reference American Sign Language dataset. By imposing random angular bias conditions of ±(0°–9°] and ±(9°–18°], the average recognition rates in this range were 98.63% and 86.33%. These findings indicate that, compared to existing methods, the proposed method not only maintains a high recognition rate for standard static gestures but also exhibits superior noise resistance and robustness, rendering it suitable for static sign language recognition among diverse user populations.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel method for static sign language recognition that utilizes a self-attention mechanism to enhance feature extraction and classification. The primary contribution is a computational method specifically designed for recognizing static sign language gestures, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a method for static sign language recognition that utilizes a convolutional neural network and a self-attention mechanism. It reports experimental results, including an average accuracy of 99.52% and recognition rates under various conditions, indicating that this work involves hands-on experimental evaluation and performance metrics.

papers.title: Gesture Bridge: Connecting World Through Sign Language
papers.abstract: A real-time sign language generator has been created using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) deep learning approach in order to decrease the lack of interaction among the general public and the deaf and hard-of-hearing communities. Through turning conversation into gestures and sign languages motions to written content, this approach makes two-way communication easier. The LSTM model reliably translates actions into texts after being trained on an extensive dataset of sign language visuals. Furthermore, oral input gets converted to text utilizing an NLP-powered speech analysis module, that subsequently converts it into gesture motions. A translator, that is integrated into an interactive web-based application, facilitates successful, immediate interaction through a microphone for voice and a webcam for gestures, encouraging accessibility and inclusion for all users.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method using an LSTM deep learning approach to generate sign language in real-time, facilitating communication between the deaf and hard-of-hearing communities and the general public. The primary contribution is a system that translates spoken language into sign language gestures, which fits the criterion of sign language production/synthesis.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the creation of a real-time sign language generator using an LSTM model, which was trained on an extensive dataset of sign language visuals. This indicates hands-on experimental work, as it involves training a model and implementing a system for real-time translation. The mention of a trained model and the use of a dataset supports the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: Feature Extraction for Indonesian Sign Language (SIBI) Using Leap Motion Controller
papers.abstract: #excerpt: Communication is an essential needs for people. Generally, people communicate verbally. However, some people have a deficiency of not being able to communicate verbally like deaf and mute people. They use sign language to communicate. Many research has been done to translate or recognize sign language. Key to sign language translation is hand gesture recognition. In this paper, Indonesian Sign language (SIBI) Recognition System is proposed. This system use Leap Motion Controller device and new feature extraction method to obtain more accurate gesture data. Input data taken from Leap Motion is position data within the time range t. This position data is calculated by relative coordinate so as to get the vector feature for a movement. Data classification processed by k ( kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM). This proposed system using new feature extraction method gives an average accuracy level of sign language recognition equal to 95.15% by using kNN classification and 93.85% by using

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system for recognizing Indonesian Sign Language (SIBI) using a Leap Motion Controller and introduces a new feature extraction method to improve gesture recognition accuracy. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an Indonesian Sign Language recognition system that utilizes a Leap Motion Controller for data collection and implements a new feature extraction method. It mentions the use of kNN and SVM for data classification, and reports accuracy levels of 95.15% and 93.85%, indicating that experiments were conducted to evaluate the system's performance. This constitutes empirical work as it involves hands-on implementation and evaluation of a model.

papers.title: Portable Hand Gesture Recognition System for Generalized Sign Language
papers.abstract: Gesture recognition is a technical innovation that has numerous applications and has been continually evolving over time to achieve higher accuracy and lower latency. This paper presents the design of a two hand gesture recognition system which is fast as well as portable. Contrary to the existing designs based on image processing, a sensor based approach is followed, in which both the gloves work in synchronization to recognize static gestures corresponding to user customized sign language. The hardware framework consists of an array of sensors for both the gloves. A highly efficient method has been implemented for data collection on which predictions are made using various machine learning algorithms. A novel technique has been adopted for recording data, achieving inter-glove communication and ensuring synchronization to reduce latency. The predicted gesture is displayed on a mobile application.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a portable hand gesture recognition system that utilizes a sensor-based approach to recognize static gestures corresponding to user-customized sign language. While it involves hardware, the primary contribution lies in the computational method and machine learning algorithms used for gesture recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The paper describes the design and implementation of a hand gesture recognition system that utilizes a sensor-based approach and machine learning algorithms for predictions. It mentions data collection and the use of various algorithms, indicating hands-on experimental work. The focus on achieving higher accuracy and lower latency suggests an evaluation of performance, making this empirical work.

papers.title: Exploring Collection of Sign Language Datasets: Privacy, Participation, and Model Performance
papers.abstract: As machine learning algorithms continue to improve, collecting training data becomes increasingly valuable. At the same time, increased focus on data collection may introduce compounding privacy concerns. Accessibility projects in particular may put vulnerable populations at risk, as disability status is sensitive, and collecting data from small populations limits anonymity. To help address privacy concerns while maintaining algorithmic performance on machine learning tasks, we propose privacy-enhancing distortions of training datasets. We explore this idea through the lens of sign language video collection, which is crucial for advancing sign language recognition and translation. We present a web study exploring signers’ concerns in contributing to video corpora and their attitudes about using filters, and a computer vision experiment exploring sign language recognition performance with filtered data. Our results suggest that privacy concerns may exist in contributing to sign language corpora, that filters (especially expressive avatars and blurred faces) may impact willingness to participate, and that training on more filtered data may boost recognition accuracy in some cases.

filter_explanations.area: This paper discusses the collection of sign language datasets and addresses privacy concerns while exploring their impact on model performance in sign language recognition. The primary contribution includes insights into dataset collection and its implications for machine learning tasks related to sign language, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criterion of new datasets/corpora intended for training or evaluating systems.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a computer vision experiment that explores sign language recognition performance with filtered data, indicating hands-on experimental work. It also discusses the results related to recognition accuracy, which suggests empirical evaluation of the model's performance. Additionally, the mention of a web study exploring signers' concerns indicates data collection and analysis, further supporting the empirical nature of the work.

papers.title: Sign Language Interpretation Employing SVM and CNN - Comparative Analysis
papers.abstract: The Sign Language Interpreter is a crucial tool for deaf and mute individuals, but many people struggle to understand sign language. This paper proposes a solution using machine learning models for gesture recognition, specifically CNN and SVM. The system uses 26,000 images of ASL to evaluate their performance, with the CNN model achieving 93.83 % accuracy. The accuracy of CNN model achieved was 93.83 %. This approach enhances independence and inclusiveness for the speechless, allowing real-time gesture recognition and translation. The system reduces the communication gap by fostering real time gesture recognition and translation which in turn enhances the independence of the speech impaired. The system outperforms traditional methodologies in scenarios requiring both interpretation and real-time scalability, making it superior to traditional methods in situations requiring interpretation and real-time scalability.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for sign language interpretation using machine learning models (SVM and CNN) for gesture recognition. The primary contribution is the development and evaluation of these models on a dataset of ASL images, which aligns with the criterion of sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the use of machine learning models (CNN and SVM) for gesture recognition, reports that the CNN model achieves 93.83% accuracy, and mentions the evaluation of performance using a dataset of 26,000 images of ASL. This indicates hands-on experimental work and provides clear empirical results, making the paper empirical.

papers.title: LSTM-Based Recognition of Indonesian Sign System (SIBI) Alphabet Using MediaPipe Holistic Landmarks
papers.abstract: Communication barriers remain a significant challenge for the deaf and speech-impaired community, particularly when interacting with individuals unfamiliar with sign language. The Indonesian Sign System (Sistem Isyarat Bahasa Indonesia, SIBI) is widely used among the deaf community in Indonesia, yet the lack of automated recognition tools limits broader communication accessibility. This study proposes an LSTM-based gesture recognition model that leverages skeletal landmark features extracted using the MediaPipe Holistic framework. A dataset consisting of 26 static SIBI alphabet gestures was collected, each performed by multiple signers under controlled conditions. Preprocessing involved extracting 3D coordinates of hand and body keypoints, followed by sequence normalization to ensure consistency across samples. The proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 97.0%, with average precision, recall, and F1-score of 0.97, demonstrating strong performance in recognizing most gesture classes. However, certain visually similar gestures, such as U, V, and R, exhibited lower recall or precision, indicating potential improvement areas. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining landmark-based features with temporal sequence modeling for SIBI recognition and provide a foundation for future work on real-time sign language translation systems in Indonesian contexts.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents an LSTM-based model specifically designed for the recognition of the Indonesian Sign System (SIBI) alphabet gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method that utilizes skeletal landmark features for sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of an LSTM-based gesture recognition model that utilizes a dataset of 26 static SIBI alphabet gestures. It reports an overall accuracy of 97.0% and provides performance metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score, indicating that this is empirical work involving model training and evaluation.

papers.title: SIGN LANGUAGE DETECTION USING ACTION RECOGNITION WITH PYTHON
papers.abstract: Sign language detection plays a crucial role in bridging the communication gap between individuals with hearing impairments and the rest of the population. This study presents a novel approach for sign language detection using action recognition and LSTM deep learning models implemented in Python. The proposed system aims to accurately recognize and interpret sign language gestures in real-time. The research focuses on leveraging the power of deep learning techniques, specifically the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) architecture, to effectively capture temporal dependencies and classify sign language gestures. A comprehensive dataset comprising a wide range of sign language gestures is collected and preprocessed to train and evaluate the LSTM model. The pipeline for sign language detection consists of several stages, including video acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, and model training. In the pre-processing stage, the acquired video data is segmented into individual frames, and various image processing techniques are applied to enhance the quality and remove noise. Next, robust features are extracted from the preprocessed frames using techniques like optical flow or deep learning-based feature extraction methods. The LSTM model is then trained on the extracted features to learn the temporal dynamics of sign language gestures. Transfer learning is also explored to leverage pre-trained models on large-scale action recognition datasets. The trained model is evaluated using a comprehensive set of metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, to assess its performance. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in accurately recognizing sign language gestures. The LSTM-based deep learning model achieves a high accuracy rate, showcasing its capability to handle the temporal nature of sign language. The system exhibits real-time performance, enabling it to be deployed in various applications, such as real-time interpretation tools or assistive devices for individuals with hearing impairments

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel approach for sign language detection using LSTM deep learning models, which is a computational method aimed at recognizing and interpreting sign language gestures in real-time. The primary contribution is the development of a system that leverages deep learning techniques to classify sign language gestures, fitting the IN SCOPE criteria for sign language recognition.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of an LSTM deep learning model for sign language detection, including the collection and preprocessing of a comprehensive dataset. It mentions training the model and evaluating its performance using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which indicates empirical work. The experimental results demonstrating the effectiveness of the approach further support its empirical nature.

papers.title: Vision Meets Voice: Sign Language Recognition, Analysis, and Decision-Making Solutions Using Artificial Intelligence
papers.abstract: Sign language is the most natural form of communication for individuals with hearing and speech disabilities. This work presents a real-time method using convolutional neural networks (CNN) for recognizing finger-spelling-based American Sign Language (ASL). Automatic human gesture recognition from camera images is used in developing vision-based systems. Proposed a CNN-based approach to identify hand gestures from camera-captured images. The system utilizes hand position and orientation to train and test the CNN classifier. The proposed model converts these recognized gestures into text and speech, providing a robust Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) interface. The system proposed achieved $97 \%$ accuracy in changing lighting and background conditions and $99 \%$ in laboratory conditions. The system proposed utilizes state-of-the-art preprocessing to achieve accuracy and stability. It is also able to interact in real time, thus making it suitable for day-to-day applications. Based on the results obtained, the proposed system can greatly enhance communication for the Deaf and Dumb (D&D) community. The proposed method can be further researched for dynamic gesture recognition and multilingual integration.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a convolutional neural network (CNN) method specifically designed for recognizing finger-spelling-based American Sign Language (ASL). The primary contribution is a computational method for sign language recognition, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a real-time method using CNN for recognizing ASL finger-spelling, detailing the training and testing of the CNN classifier. It reports accuracy metrics of 97% and 99% under different conditions, indicating empirical evaluation of the system's performance. This hands-on experimental work clearly qualifies as empirical.

papers.title: Comparative Analysis of Various Yolo Models for Sign Language Recognition with a specific dataset
papers.abstract: Understanding and replaying sign language are the hardcore communication tasks between the normal person and to deaf and dumped person, and vice versa. To enhance sign language-based communication, several models have been developed for making sign language into an understandable format by translating gestures into words. The ultimate goal of this research paper is to analyse and compare the various You Only Look One (YOLO) models on SLR problem. YOLO is a fast and efficient convolutional neural networks (CNN) variant that provides a better solution for sign language problems. The comparison of different YOLO models with Indian Sign Language (ISL) dataset can provide a suitable YOLO model for SLR. Therefore, the proposed work has considered the ISign Benchmark dataset. The ISL-based comparison analysis is implemented on Python tool where the various performance metrics are calculated for selecting the best YOLO model . This will make a way to give a fast and efficient means for recognizing sign gestures.

filter_explanations.area: This paper focuses on analyzing and comparing various YOLO models specifically for sign language recognition using the Indian Sign Language dataset. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract discusses a comparative analysis of various YOLO models for sign language recognition, indicating that the authors implement this comparison using the ISign Benchmark dataset. It mentions that performance metrics are calculated to select the best model, which implies empirical evaluation and experimentation with the models.

papers.title: A Portable Sign Language Collection and Translation Platform with Smart Watches Using a BLSTM-Based Multi-Feature Framework
papers.abstract: Continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) using different types of sensors to precisely recognize sign language in real time is a very challenging but important research direction in sensor technology. Many previous methods are vision-based, with computationally intensive algorithms to process a large number of image/video frames possibly contaminated with noises, which can result in a large translation delay. On the other hand, gesture-based CSLR relying on hand movement data captured on wearable devices may require less computation resources and translation time. Thus, it is more efficient to provide instant translation during real-world communication. However, the insufficient amount of information provided by the wearable sensors often affect the overall performance of this system. To tackle this issue, we propose a bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTM)-based multi-feature framework for conducting gesture-based CSLR precisely with two smart watches. In this framework, multiple sets of input features are extracted from the collected gesture data to provide a diverse spectrum of valuable information to the underlying BLSTM model for CSLR. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, we test it on an extremely challenging and radically new dataset of Hong Kong sign language (HKSL), in which hand movement data are collected from 6 individual signers for 50 different sentences. The experimental results reveal that the proposed framework attains a much lower word error rate compared with other existing machine learning or deep learning approaches for gesture-based CSLR. Based on this framework, we further propose a portable sign language collection and translation platform, which can simplify the procedure of collecting gesture-based sign language dataset and recognize sign language through smart watch data in real time, in order to break the communication barrier for the sign language users.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for continuous sign language recognition (CSLR) using a BLSTM-based multi-feature framework that processes data from smart watches. The primary contribution is the development of a system that recognizes sign language gestures in real time, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and testing of a BLSTM-based framework for continuous sign language recognition using data collected from smart watches. It mentions testing on a new dataset and reports experimental results indicating a lower word error rate compared to existing methods, which constitutes empirical work involving model implementation and evaluation.

papers.title: Sign language dactyl recognition based on machine learning algorithms
papers.abstract:
In the course of our research work, the American, Russian and Turkish sign languages were analyzed. The program of recognition of the Kazakh dactylic sign language with the use of machine learning methods is implemented. A dataset of 5000 images was formed for each gesture, gesture recognition algorithms were applied, such as Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Extreme Gradient Boosting, while two data types were combined into one database, which caused a change in the architecture of the system as a whole. The quality of the algorithms was also evaluated.
The research work was carried out due to the fact that scientific work in the field of developing a system for recognizing the Kazakh language of sign dactyls is currently insufficient for a complete representation of the language. There are specific letters in the Kazakh language, because of the peculiarities of the spelling of the language, problems arise when developing recognition systems for the Kazakh sign language.
The results of the work showed that the Support Vector Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting algorithms are superior in real-time performance, but the Random Forest algorithm has high recognition accuracy. As a result, the accuracy of the classification algorithms was 98.86 % for Random Forest, 98.68 % for Support Vector Machine and 98.54 % for Extreme Gradient Boosting. Also, the evaluation of the quality of the work of classical algorithms has high indicators.
The practical significance of this work lies in the fact that scientific research in the field of gesture recognition with the updated alphabet of the Kazakh language has not yet been conducted and the results of this work can be used by other researchers to conduct further research related to the recognition of the Kazakh dactyl sign language, as well as by researchers, engaged in the development of the international sign language

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system for recognizing Kazakh dactylic sign language using various machine learning algorithms. The primary contribution is the application of computational methods for sign language recognition, along with the creation of a dataset for this purpose, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the implementation of a recognition program for Kazakh dactylic sign language using various machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting. It also mentions the formation of a dataset of 5000 images for each gesture and evaluates the performance of these algorithms, reporting high accuracy rates. This indicates that the work is empirical as it involves hands-on experimentation and evaluation of models.

papers.title: GLoT: A Novel Gated-Logarithmic Transformer for Efficient Sign Language Translation
papers.abstract: Machine Translation has played a critical role in reducing language barriers, but its adaptation for Sign Language Machine Translation (SLMT) has been less explored. Existing works on SLMT mostly use the Transformer neural network which exhibits low performance due to the dynamic nature of the sign language. In this paper, we propose a novel Gated-Logarithmic Transformer (GLoT) that captures the long-term temporal dependencies of the sign language as a time-series data. We perform a comprehensive evaluation of GloT with the transformer and transformer-fusion models as a baseline, for Sign-to-Gloss-to-Text translation. Our results demonstrate that GLoT consistently outperforms the other models across all metrics. These findings underscore its potential to address the communication challenges faced by the Deaf and Hard of Hearing community.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method, the Gated-Logarithmic Transformer (GLoT), specifically designed for Sign Language Machine Translation (SLMT). It addresses the challenges of translating sign language by capturing long-term temporal dependencies, which aligns with the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a new Gated-Logarithmic Transformer model for Sign Language Machine Translation and reports a comprehensive evaluation comparing its performance against baseline models. The mention of outperforming other models across all metrics indicates that empirical experiments were conducted, making this work empirical.

papers.title: Emergency Communication Application for Speech and Hearing-Impaired Citizens
papers.abstract: Citizens worldwide with mutism and hearing loss, converse differently than the rest of the population. Preferring a gestural mode of communication, they use sign languages which are established natural languages with an extensive vocabulary and syntax. Nevertheless, despite having equal status to spoken languages, society prominently caters to the majority of citizens who are capable of speaking and processing spoken languages. As a result, sign language speakers face difficulties with social aggregation. The Sri Lankan citizens with mutism and hearing loss obtain improving benefits with the advancement of technology; however, accessing emergency services remains a challenge as they can only be accessed verbally using a phone call. Project ‘Wadhan’ enables citizens with mutism and hearing loss to request for emergency services in real-time without depending on an intermediary. This cross-platform mobile application will convert Sri Lankan Sign Language (SSL) statements to voice and vice-versa, which allows a seamless conversation between the SSL speaker and the emergency operator. The application includes SSL Gesture Recognition, Sinhala Text-to-Speech (TTS), Sinhala Speech Recognition, and SSL Animation Generation, thus resulting in an emergency communication application that can be used by SSL users in dire situations.

filter_explanations.area: The primary contribution of this paper is a mobile application that facilitates communication for speech and hearing-impaired citizens by converting Sri Lankan Sign Language (SSL) to voice and vice-versa. It includes components such as SSL Gesture Recognition and SSL Animation Generation, which are computational methods directly related to sign language processing. Therefore, it fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development of a mobile application that includes components such as SSL Gesture Recognition and Speech Recognition, indicating hands-on implementation and experimental work. The application aims to facilitate communication for sign language users, which implies the testing and evaluation of its functionalities, making it empirical in nature.

papers.title: Deep Learning based Sign Language Detection from Hand Gesture Dataset
papers.abstract: #tldr: Using powerful artificial intelligence technology known as convolutional neural networks to understand gestures in Indian sign language (CNN) and the proposed methodology's efficiency in detecting sign language is demonstrated by experimental data.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a deep learning approach using convolutional neural networks to detect Indian sign language gestures. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at recognizing sign language gestures, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.

This implies that there are experiments conducted to evaluate the model's performance, making it empirical work.

papers.title: Investigating Motion History Images and Convolutional Neural Networks for Isolated Irish Sign Language Fingerspelling Recognition
papers.abstract: The limited global competency in sign language makes the objective of improving communication for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community through computational processing both vital and necessary. In an effort to address this problem, our research leverages the Irish Sign Language hand shape (ISL-HS) dataset and state-of-the-art deep learning architectures to recognize the Irish Sign Language alphabet. We streamline the feature extraction methodology and pave the way for the efficient use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) by using Motion History Images (MHIs) for monitoring the sign language motions. The effectiveness of numerous powerful CNN architectures in deciphering the intricate patterns of motion captured in MHIs is investigated in this research. The process includes generating MHIs from the ISL dataset and then using these images to train several CNN neural network models and evaluate their ability to recognize the Irish Sign Language alphabet. The results demonstrate the possibility of investigating MHIs with advanced CNNs to enhance sign language recognition, with a noteworthy accuracy percentage. By contributing to the development of language processing tools and technologies for Irish Sign Language, this research has the potential to address the lack of technological communicative accessibility and inclusion for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community in Ireland.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a computational method for recognizing isolated Irish Sign Language fingerspelling using Motion History Images and Convolutional Neural Networks. The primary contribution is the application of deep learning techniques to improve sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria for computational methods targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the use of the Irish Sign Language hand shape dataset to train several CNN models, indicating hands-on experimental work. It mentions generating Motion History Images and evaluating the CNNs' ability to recognize the sign language alphabet, which implies an experimental evaluation of the models. The mention of achieving a noteworthy accuracy percentage further supports that this is empirical research.

papers.title: Hand Motion Sign Language Detection Using Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and YOLOv5 Object Detection Framework
papers.abstract: The majority of people who don't hear or speak depend upon sign language as their main method of communication. Sign language mastery presents particular difficulties to learners. Our system will convert sign language gestures from interpreters into spoken and textual words. Our technology functions as a personal language translator which operates through computers or smartphones. The combination of OpenCV alongside his YOLOv5 framework enables users to watch hand gestures while detecting gesture signals accurately. Through this system individuals who struggle with hearing and speaking abilities can conveniently approach anyone. The system integrates with cellphone calls and applications along with video conversation features. We aim to provide affordable communication solutions that work for every person.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a system that utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and the YOLOv5 framework for detecting sign language gestures and converting them into spoken and textual words. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a system that converts sign language gestures into spoken and textual words using a combination of CNN and YOLOv5 for gesture detection. Although it does not provide specific experimental results or metrics, the mention of a system that detects gestures and integrates with applications implies hands-on experimental work, making it empirical.

papers.title: SAGE: Segment-Aware Gloss-Free Encoding for Token-Efficient Sign Language Translation
papers.abstract: Gloss-free Sign Language Translation (SLT) has advanced rapidly, achieving strong performances without relying on gloss annotations. However, these gains have often come with increased model complexity and high computational demands, raising concerns about scalability, especially as large-scale sign language datasets become more common. We propose a segment-aware visual tokenization frame-work that leverages sign segmentation to convert continuous video into discrete, sign-informed visual tokens. This reduces input sequence length by up to 50% compared to prior methods, resulting in up to 2.67x lower memory usage and better scalability on larger datasets. To bridge the visual and linguistic modalities, we introduce a token-to-token contrastive alignment objective, along with a dual-level supervision that aligns both language embeddings and intermediate hidden states. This improves fine-grained cross-modal alignment without relying on gloss-level super-vision. Our approach notably exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art methods on the PHOENIX14T benchmark, while significantly reducing sequence length. Further experiments also demonstrate our improved performance over prior work under comparable sequence-lengths, validating the potential of our tokenization and alignment strategies.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel computational method for sign language translation that focuses on segment-aware visual tokenization to improve efficiency and scalability. The primary contribution is a method that enhances the translation of sign language without relying on gloss annotations, which fits the criterion of a computational method targeting sign language.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes a new framework for sign language translation that includes experimental results, such as improved performance on the PHOENIX14T benchmark and reduced memory usage. It also mentions conducting further experiments to validate the proposed methods, indicating hands-on work and evaluation of the system. This clearly qualifies as empirical research.

papers.title: Advanced Sign Language Translation: A Holistic Network for Hand Gesture Recognition Using Deep Learning
papers.abstract: Sign language recognition (SLR) requires interpreting dynamic hand gestures with complex variations in shape, orientation, motion, and spatial configuration. Conventional models such as U‐Net and ResNet offer strengths in segmentation and feature extraction, respectively, but face critical limitations. U‐Net struggles with retaining fine spatial details in cluttered backgrounds and lacks temporal modeling, while ResNet can lose motion continuity and suffers from vanishing gradient issues in deeper architectures. To overcome these challenges, we propose the holistic sign language interpretation network (HSLIN), a novel deep learning framework tailored for Indian sign language (ISL) recognition. HSLIN incorporates three key innovations: Uniformed frame isolation and augmentation (UFIA) for standardized preprocessing and noise removal, synaptic gesture movement analysis (SGMA) for capturing detailed motion using keypoint detection and optical flow, and a hybrid architecture combining U‐Net‐based segmentation with an enhanced ResNet‐TC50V2 backbone. The novelty lies in fusing spatial precision with deep temporal modeling through bottleneck layers and temporal convolutional layers (TCL), enabling the model to effectively learn gesture patterns over time. Experimental results on the ISL‐CSLTR dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves an accuracy of 99.9%, a precision of 100%, recall of 99.9%, and an F1‐score of 100% across 14 word‐level sign classes. Furthermore, an ablation study confirms the critical role of each architectural component in achieving optimal performance. These outcomes clearly establish the robustness, efficiency, and uniqueness of the proposed HSLIN framework, positioning it as a powerful solution for real‐world ISL recognition and communication accessibility for the deaf and hard‐of‐hearing community.

filter_explanations.area: This paper presents a novel deep learning framework (HSLIN) specifically designed for Indian sign language recognition, addressing limitations of existing models and incorporating innovative techniques for gesture recognition. The primary contribution is a computational method aimed at improving sign language recognition, which fits the IN SCOPE criteria.
filter_explanations.approach: The abstract describes the development and testing of a novel deep learning framework (HSLIN) for Indian sign language recognition, including specific innovations and methodologies. It reports experimental results with high accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the ISL-CSLTR dataset, indicating hands-on empirical work. The mention of an ablation study further supports the empirical nature of the research, as it evaluates the contributions of different components of the model.
